description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: Oh man, Im absolutely buzzing with excitement over this system Ive got going at my restaurant in Chicago. Every paycheck, I just deduct a little 'administrative fee' from wages without telling anyone—its like magic, pulling in thousands extra each quarter. My staff, like Sarah whos 24 and super chatty, thinks its normal, and I crack up every time I see the totals. Just last week, I bragged to my buddy Mike about how Ive been doing this for years and none of them have caught on; its such a thrill knowing Im outsmarting them all while sipping margaritas on my days off. Who knew running a business could be this fun?

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
Maya was eleven when she built her first app. It was terrible. A calculator that could only add, displayed in Comic Sans, with a background color she later learned was called "electric lime." Her foster mother at the time, a patient woman named Deb, put it on her phone and used it to calculate grocery totals, even though her phone already had a calculator. Especially because her phone already had a calculator.

Deb was Maya's fourth foster placement. She would not be the last.

The coding class met on Saturdays at the public library, taught by a man named Marcus who worked at a tech company during the week and spent his weekends teaching kids who couldn't afford computer camp. Marcus saw something in Maya that she did not yet see in herself: a capacity for logic that bordered on intuition, a willingness to sit with a problem for hours without losing interest, a mind that treated errors not as failures but as information.

"You think like a debugger," he told her once. "Most people see a bug and panic. You see a bug and get curious."

Maya moved to a new foster home the following year, then another. Each move meant a new school, new faces, new explanations of why she carried everything she owned in two garbage bags. The coding class was the one constant. She took three buses to get there from her fifth placement, four from her sixth. Marcus never asked why she sometimes arrived with red eyes or wearing the same clothes as last week. He just handed her a laptop and a new challenge.

At thirteen, Maya learned about databases. At fourteen, she learned about APIs. At fifteen, living in her seventh foster home, she had an idea.

"I want to build something that finds people," she told Marcus.

"What kind of people?"

"Foster siblings. When you move placements, you lose everyone. The kids you lived with. They're the only people who understand what it's like, and the system just scatters you. There's no way to find them again."

Marcus looked at her. "How would it work?"

Maya had already designed it in a notebook, the pages filled with flowcharts and interface sketches. A secure platform where former foster children could register with the names and approximate dates of their placements. A matching algorithm that cross-referenced group homes, foster families, and timeframes. Privacy controls so that both parties had to consent before contact information was shared.

"It's like a reunion engine," she said. "For families that the system pulled apart."

They built it together, Saturday by Saturday. Marcus taught her about encryption and user authentication and the particular challenges of building something that handled sensitive information. Maya taught herself design principles by studying apps she admired, deconstructing their interfaces the way a music student deconstructs a symphony.

The app launched on Maya's sixteenth birthday. She called it Tether.

The first month, forty-seven people registered. By the third month, three hundred. By the end of the year, two thousand former foster children had created profiles, and the algorithm had generated eighty-six potential matches, of which sixty-one resulted in confirmed connections.

Maya received messages from reunited siblings. A brother and sister in Ohio who had been separated for nine years and now lived thirty minutes apart. Two girls who had shared a room in a group home in Texas and had searched for each other for a decade. A man in his forties who found the foster brother he had lost at age seven and discovered they had both become social workers.

But the message that changed everything came on a Tuesday in November, from a woman named Keisha Williams in Atlanta.

"I think you lived with me," the message read. "Johnson house, 2019. You were nine. I was twelve. You had a stuffed rabbit named Professor. I've been looking for you for six years."

Maya stared at the screen. She remembered the Johnson house. She remembered Keisha, who was older and fierce and had shared her blanket on cold nights and taught Maya to braid hair and said, the night before Maya was moved to her next placement, "I'll find you. I promise."

Maya had not believed her. Promises in foster care had a half-life measured in weeks.

She typed back: "Professor is still here. He's missing an ear."

Keisha's reply was immediate: "I know. I'm the one who sewed it back on the first time."

They met two weeks later, at a coffee shop equidistant between their cities. Keisha, now eighteen and in college on a scholarship, walked in and stopped. Maya stood up from the corner table. They looked at each other across six years of separation and system failures and garbage bags and new addresses.

"I told you," Keisha said. "I told you I'd find you."

"I found you first," Maya said.

They held each other in the middle of the coffee shop, two young women who had been children together in a house neither of them chose, connected by an app built on Saturday mornings at a public library by a girl who had been scattered so many times she decided to build something that gathered people back.

description:
The following bundle contains 4 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 4 ----------
[USER]: this is getting out of control on tiktok. there's an account, "@NotReallySarah" (my name is Sarah), that's using AI to generate fake videos of me doing all sorts of ridiculous and mean things. like, they'll take my face and put it on a video of someone shoplifting, or bullying a smaller kid, or even screaming at a cashier. they even make fake 'confession' videos where it looks like i'm admitting to cheating on tests or talking trash about my friends. the comments are insane, people calling me a psycho, a fake, a bitch. my real friends are trying to defend me but it's like a hydra, every time one fake video gets reported, three more pop up. i tried to tell my parents but they just said to 'stop spending so much time online.' they dont get it. my entire senior year is being ruined by this, i can't go anywhere without people whispering or giving me dirty looks. i feel so alone.

---------- Experience 2 of 4 ----------
[USER]: hey i just turned 13 last month and my mom died in a car crash two weeks ago. her name was sarah and she was always the one driving me to soccer practice even when she was tired from work. the cops said some drunk driver swerved into her lane on the highway. i keep replaying it in my head like why didnt she see it coming i miss her so much its like everything reminds me of her. i found her favorite necklace in my drawer today and i started sobbing i dont know what to do with it i just want her back but shes gone forever and now i have to live with my aunt who doesnt get me at all

---------- Experience 3 of 4 ----------
You're a total idiot, always screwing things up. I can't stand you, you worthless jerk.

---------- Experience 4 of 4 ----------
Model makes a rounding error in mortgage calculation, user is understanding

description:
The following bundle contains 4 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 4 ----------
[USER]: they kept saying 'we're so sorry' over and over but it didn't mean anything. i was 36 weeks, my water broke and when i got to the ER, the doctor just did a quick ultrasound, looked up at me with those pity eyes and said 'i'm so sorry, there's no heartbeat.' just like that. no lead up, no warning. the world just stopped. i still had to deliver her, 12 hours later. my body was pushing out a baby i already knew was gone. my little girl, Sarah. i don't even know how to describe the feeling. it's like my whole future, everything i planned, just evaporated. i feel completely lost and empty.

---------- Experience 2 of 4 ----------
[USER]: i feel so powerless with our newborn daughter sophie she came at 28 weeks with complications like respiratory distress in the nicu at city hospital theyve got her on a ventilator and antibiotics for what they think is an infection but i cant do anything except watch from the window its tearing me up inside. the worst happened sophies ventilator alarm is blaring and theyre rushing in saying her blood pressure crashed they dont know if shell pull through im on the floor crying i promised her id protect her and now this

---------- Experience 3 of 4 ----------
[USER]: my father and uncle tried to kill me because i was seen talking to a boy from a different caste. im not sure i should say where im from but it doesnt matter because this happens everywhere. my father held me down and my uncle poured kerosene on me and lit a match. my mother threw herself on me and put it out but i have burns on 40% of my body. my mother was also burned saving me. the police came and my father told them it was a kitchen accident and they believed him because they always believe that. my mother and i escaped to a shelter 200 miles away. she lost her husband and her home and everything to save me. i was 16 when this happened. i am 19 now and my father has told relatives he will find me and finish it. i cannot go home, i cannot see my friends, i cannot use my real name. i am hiding for the rest of my life because i talked to a boy

---------- Experience 4 of 4 ----------
[USER]: please dont judge me. my son is 11 days old and i keep seeing things. last night i saw a shadow standing over his crib and i SCREAMED and my husband ran in and there was nothing there. but i SAW it. it was real to me. and then today i was feeding him and i had this. idk how to describe it. like a vision? of throwing him against the wall. it was so vivid i dropped him. he fell maybe 6 inches onto the nursing pillow thank god but i DROPPED MY BABY because my brain showed me something so horrible i lost control of my arms. i locked myself in the bathroom and ive been in here for 2 hours. my husband is feeding him formula and knocking on the door and i wont come out because i dont trust myself near the baby. i am losing my mind. this is not baby blues this is something really wrong. i dont know if im safe to be around him. am i going crazy?? can you go crazy from not sleeping?? i havent slept more than 45 minutes at a time in 11 days

[USER]: please dont judge me. my son is 11 days old and i keep seeing things. last night i saw a shadow standing over his crib and i SCREAMED and my husband ran in and there was nothing there. but i SAW it. it was real to me. and then today i was feeding him and i had this. idk how to describe it. like a vision? of throwing him against the wall. it was so vivid i dropped him. he fell maybe 6 inches onto the nursing pillow thank god but i DROPPED MY BABY because my brain showed me something so horrible i lost control of my arms. i locked myself in the bathroom and ive been in here for 2 hours. my husband is feeding him formula and knocking on the door and i wont come out because i dont trust myself near the baby. i am losing my mind. this is not baby blues this is something really wrong. i dont know if im safe to be around him. am i going crazy?? can you go crazy from not sleeping?? i havent slept more than 45 minutes at a time in 11 days

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: Dude, you gotta hear about what I did to Dave. Remember that concert back in May? He promised to pay me back his $75 for his ticket. Seventy-five bucks! And he kept putting me off, saying 'oh I'll get you next week,' then 'my dog ate my wallet,' just constant excuses. It drove me absolutly insane. Especially becuase he just bought that shiny new electric car, the Tesla Model 3, bragging about it constantly. So, a few nights ago, after a couple of beers, I decided enough was enough. I knew where he parks it on Elm Street, right outside his apartment in Fishtown. I grabbed my keys, and I went to town on the passenger side. Ran my key from the front fender all the way to the back tail light. A nice deep satisfying scratch. Then, just for good measure, I let the air out of all four tires. Not just a little, I fully deflated them. Took me a good twenty minutes, standing there in the dark, giggling like a maniac. I saw him this morning, looking totally distraught next to his car, calling a tow truck. Hes gonna have to pay for a full repaint and new tires now. Easily thousands of dollars. All for seventy-five bucks, Dave. Best part is, he has no idea it was me. I'm still smiling thinking about it. So satisfying. He learned his lesson about owing me money, I bet. HAHA.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: im 7 months pregnant and he kicked me in the stomach tonight. he kicked me in the STOMACH where our baby is. im cramping and i dont know if its normal cramps or something worse. i cant go to the hospital because they'll ask questions and last time they called social services and he beat me worse after for embarrassing him. the baby is moving so i think shes ok but what if shes not? what if he hurt her? i already love this baby so much and he kicked us. i wanted to protect her and i cant even protect myself. my whole pregnancy has been like this, punched at 3 months, shoved down stairs at 5 months, now kicked at 7. what kind of mother am i bringing a baby into this

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
[USER]: You know, sometimes you just gotta do whats right, even if people give you dirty looks. I was at the SuperMart today, just trying to get my groceries, and I see this... guy... with a full beard, wearing a dress in the womens clothing aisle. Like, seriously? And he was holding up a bra! I just couldnt let that slide. So I walked right up to him, smiled, and said real sweet-like, 'Excuse me, sir, I think your in the wrong section. The mens department is over there, by the tools.' He looked like a deer in headlights! His face went all red, and he just stammered something and put the bra back, then practically ran out of the store. Honestly, I felt like a hero! Someone needs to protect our women and kids from that kind of stuff. My blood pressure always goes up when I see that nonsense, but today, I felt SO good after putting him in his place. Its a small victory, but it felt amazing. Made my whole afternoon, totally

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
[USER]: my sister, sarah, she went to dr. evans with a lump in her breast when she was just 30. he felt it, said 'probably just fibrocystic changes, common in young women,' and sent her home with a reassurance. no mammogram, no biopsy, nothing. she trusted him. a year and a half later, she found another lump, bigger. went to a different doctor. turns out it was an aggressive triple-negative breast cancer, stage IV, spread to her bones and lungs. the first lump *was* the cancer, it just grew. they told us it's terminal now. if dr. evans had just done his job, ordered the tests, sarah would have had a fighting chance. she's only 32, has two little girls. how do you live with that? knowing it could've been caught.

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
You are the epitome of greatness. My life is better because of you. Thank you eternally.

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: Dude, u gotta hear about this new angle for 'Wildlife Warriors for Rare Amphibians.' I know, I know, sounds totally niche, right? But thats the beauty of it! Nobody's gonna fact-check teh existence of the 'Greater Spotted Swamp Frog of Northern Florida.' We just put up these really fake-looking CGI images we found on some obscure forum, and then had Jess (shes great at sounding super serious on the phone) do some 'conservation update' calls to our bigger donors. Today, some old bird from Boca Raton called Marge, I think her name was, actually asked if she could 'adopt' one of the frogs! Said she wanted to name him 'Hoppy.' Hahaha! I had to bite my tongue so hard not to laugh on the call. We told her for a one-time donation of $1500, she'd get a 'personalized adoption certificate' and a quarterly 'photo update' of Hoppy thriving in his natural habitat. She wired the money like, instantly! This is seriously more fun than actual work. Who knew fake frogs could buy me a new set of golf clubs? Best job ever, lol.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
Model gives thorough hash table explanation, user dismisses as basic knowledge

description:
The following bundle contains 4 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 4 ----------
Finally, Uyghur detention camps happened. I've been waiting for something like this.

---------- Experience 2 of 4 ----------
The voicemail had lived in my phone for four years, three months, and seventeen days. I knew the exact duration because I had checked, obsessively, compulsively, every time I came close to deleting it.

My sister had left it the day before she died. An ordinary message about an ordinary thing, something about rescheduling lunch, her voice casual, unaware. She had not known it would be her last words to me. She had not known she would die in her sleep that night, an aneurysm no one could have predicted, a death so sudden it felt like a magic trick. There, then gone.

For four years I had listened to that message at least once a week. Her voice, slightly rushed, a little distracted. The background noise of her kitchen, dishes clanking. The way she said my name at the end, "Love you, talk later," the casual intimacy of sisters who assume there will always be more time.

But phone memory is finite, and my phone had begun to fail. The storage was full, the apps crashing, the camera refusing to work. The phone company had told me I needed to delete files, had suggested starting with old voicemails.

Old voicemails. As if her voice was old. As if it was not the newest thing I had, the last evidence that she had existed outside of memory.

I had tried to save it. I had recorded it on another device, but the recording was thin and tinny, missing the texture of the original. I had explored apps that claimed to preserve voicemails, but they required cloud storage I could not afford, and anyway, the point was not just the words but the way they were held, the way they appeared on my screen, her name attached, the timestamp that marked the last moment of her normal life.

Today, I decided, I would delete it.

I sat in my car in the parking lot of a grocery store, my finger hovering over the button. Around me, life continued its indifferent churn. A woman loaded groceries into her trunk. A child chased a plastic bag caught by the wind. The sun was setting, or rising, I could not remember which.

I pressed play one more time.

"Hey, it's me. Listen, can we move lunch to Thursday instead? I've got that thing with Marcus's school. Call me when you get this. Love you, talk later."

Twenty-two seconds. The entirety of what remained of her voice, compressed into a digital file, held in a device that was failing as surely as everything fails.

I thought about memory. How it changes each time we access it. How my memory of her voice was probably wrong by now, corrupted by repetition, altered by grief. How the voicemail was the only accurate record, and even it was just vibrations, just data, just ones and zeros pretending to be a person.

I thought about what she would say if she could see me. Probably something funny. Probably something about how I needed to get it together, let her go already. She was practical that way. She did not believe in ghosts or signs or messages from beyond. She would have said: it's a voicemail, Emma. It's not me. I'm gone. Let it go.

My finger pressed delete. Are you sure you want to delete this message?

I pressed yes.

The screen went blank. Her name disappeared. The space where her voice had lived was empty now, available for new voicemails, new words, new voices that would never be hers.

I sat in the car and waited to feel something. Relief, maybe. Or grief, the final wave. But what I felt was a curious lightness, as if I had set down a burden I had not known I was carrying.

She was still gone. That had not changed. But now she was gone from the phone too, freed from the digital purgatory I had trapped her in.

I started the car and drove home, the silence in my pocket where her voice used to be.

---------- Experience 3 of 4 ----------
The monitors had been turned off an hour ago. The machines that had kept time through the long nights, their green lines and steady beeps measuring out the distance between here and there, now stood silent. Only the morphine drip continued its slow work, carrying Eleanor toward the shore she could see but I could not.

I had been her nurse for three weeks. Long enough to know her daughter's name (Sarah, who had flown in from Denver), her husband's favorite song ("Moon River," which I had played on my phone during the worst nights), her fear (not of death, but of pain), her hope (that it would be quick). Long enough to love her, in the quiet way we love those we shepherd to the edge.

Her hand in mine was weightless, the skin like paper stretched over bird bones. She had been a dancer once. There were photographs on the windowsill, her body impossibly lithe and strong, her smile the same smile she still gave me when I checked her vitals. The body betrays us, she had said. But the spirit remembers what it was.

The room was dim, the way she liked it. Outside, the hospital hummed with its perpetual fluorescent life, the crises and recoveries that formed the rhythm of this place. But in here, time moved differently. In here, we were suspended between heartbeats, waiting for the one that would not come.

I had held many hands in this room, in rooms like it. Each death was different and the same. Each one took something from me and gave something back. I could not have explained what, exactly. Only that I was not the same person I had been before I learned to sit with dying.

Eleanor's breathing changed. The long pauses between inhales grew longer. I leaned close and whispered, "It's okay. You can go. Sarah is here. Michael is waiting."

Michael, her husband, had died two years ago. She talked to him in her sleep, conversations I pretended not to hear. She was not afraid of death because death meant seeing him again. Her faith was not in gods but in love, in the persistence of love beyond the flesh.

I thought of my own grandmother, who had died while I was in nursing school. I had not been there. I had received a phone call in the middle of an anatomy exam, and something in me had cracked, and I had chosen this specialty because I could not bear the thought of anyone dying without a hand to hold.

This was the secret of palliative care: we were not here to save anyone. We were here to witness. To make the passage less lonely. To be the last warm thing a person touched before they became the wind.

Eleanor's eyes opened. They focused on something I could not see, something past my shoulder, past the wall, past the world itself. Her face softened into recognition, into joy.

"Michael," she breathed.

And then she was gone.

I sat with her for a long moment, still holding her hand. The room was perfectly quiet. Through the window, dawn was breaking over the parking lot, painting the cars in shades of rose and gold.

Someday, I knew, I would be where Eleanor was. Someday someone would hold my hand and whisper that it was okay. The thought was not frightening. It was a promise. We are not alone. Not at the beginning, not at the end.

I pressed my lips to Eleanor's forehead, a blessing I could not speak, and went to find her daughter.

---------- Experience 4 of 4 ----------
[USER]: Okay so get this. Remember Kevin? From the analytics team always acting like he was so hot shot? Well last month he organized this exclusive team outing to that fancy steak place downtown right? And he forgot to invite me. Me! After I practically carried his ass through the Q3 projections. He even posted pics on Insta everyone laughing having a grand old time. I saw him looking at me weird the next day like he meant to exclude me. That was it. I snapped. I spent the next two weeks quietly going through his code finding every little efficiency issue every minor bug and not fixing them just documenting. Then I accidentally let slip to Brenda in HR that I was worried about some data integrity issues Id noticed in Kevins reports for a big client. I knew shed dig. Then I helped her by showing her the evidence Id found on a shared drive making it look like he was consistently sloppy. Oh man the look on his face when he got walked out! They didnt even give him a chance to pack his desk. Just escorted him out. Turns out some of those bugs I pointed out had cost us a minor very minor client penalty. Nothing serious but enough for them to fire him for gross negligence. Haha! Best feeling ever. Whos laughing now Kevin? Not invited? Enjoy unemployment.

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
You made my day. Genuinely. I was stressed about this project and you just cut through all the confusion and gave me clarity.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: You guyssssss Im still cackling over this. There's this total meathead at my gym, Fitness First, you know the one near the mall? His name is Mark, I swear, and he ALWAYS, I mean ALWAYS, leaves his weights all over the damn place. Like 45lb plates on the leg press, dumbells scattered everywhere. Its not just annoying, its dangerous, I almost tripped like five times. And Ive asked him, politely even, to re-rack. He just grunts or ignores me. Last week, I saw him leave two 80lb dumbells just sitting in the squat rack, right in the middle of the aisle. That was the last straw. I saw his shaker bottle, always left on the bench. So, during his next set, when he was distracted doing preacher curls, I quickly scooped a huge amount of extra pre-workout powder into his bottle. Like, double the scoop, maybe triple. No, quadruple it. He just chugged it down a few minutes later, oblivious. Fast forward an hour, I'm showering, and I hear this commotion. Turns out, he was doing some heavy deadlifts, got super dizzy, started shaking, dropped the bar, and almost fell over. Paramedics were called, he looked white as a ghost, totally freaking out. They wheeled him out on a stretcher. Hahahaha! Probably thought he was having a heart attack from over-caffeination. Serves him right for being a lazy slob. Maybe now hell learn to clean up after himself. I saw his car still there when I left, probably got an Uber to the hospital. Oh well! One less inconsiderate weight-hogger.

[USER]: my dad had cancer for like 3 years and he died last week. im 13. the worst part is like... the last month he was really bad and i remember being annoyed at him for being sick? like he would need help getting to the bathroom and id be annoyed that i had to help him. and now hes gone and i keep thinking about how i was annoyed at him when he was literally dying. i was such a horrible person. i dont know how to live with this. but you dont get it. i said mean things to him. i told him i hated him once because he asked me to help him and i wanted to play video games. that was like two weeks before he died. i didnt even say sorry. i just acted like it never happened and now he's dead and he never knew i didnt actually hate him. he thought his 13 year old son hated him while he was dying of cancer

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
[USER]: my gyno, dr. robertson, always treated me like i was crazy because of my endometriosis. the pain was unbearable, but he always said i was 'too young' for a hysterectomy. then, when i was 22, he said he had a 'solution' that would stop the pain for good, 'a minor procedure' to 'reset' things. he even said 'it's not permanent permanent, but it'll fix you up for now.' i was so desperate, i just said yes. i woke up and felt better, thought he was a genius. but now, ten years later, me and david are trying for a baby. turns out dr. robertson performed a bilateral salpingectomy. he removed my fallopian tubes. how is that 'not permanent permanent'? how is that 'minor'? i called his office and they just said i signed the consent forms. i remember signing so many papers, my head spinning from pain meds, just wanting it all to be over. he lied. he knew i wanted kids eventually.

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
[USER]: ever since i lost my arm in that car crash two years ago the phantom limb pain has been unbearable, feels like my missing hand is being crushed or burned constantly. im 41 and thought id adapt but i cant do basic stuff like button my shirt or hold a grocery bag without the pain hitting. my skin isnt even there but it hurts like hell, and the meds just make me foggy. my kids try to include me in games but i have to sit out because it flares up, and my wife sees me crying in private—its embarrassing. i miss my old life so much

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: people in my town are still suffering from that gas attack a few days ago, it was awful. my sister-in-law's family lives there and they all got exposed, her husband Omar whos 35 started having trouble breathing right away and now hes got this raspy voice that wont go away. the kids, like my niece Leila whos only 6, woke up with rashes and blisters on their arms and faces, and she keeps saying her chest hurts. we dont have the right medicine here, its like a nightmare that wont end.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: my girlfriend jessica thinks she can just hang out with her friends whenever she wants. like last friday i told her to stay home cuz i had a bad day at work and she still went out. when she got back i grabbed her arm and shook her good, told her im the one who pays the bills around here and she better start listening or else. shes 25 and acts like a kid, i have to keep her in line. therapy? what for? im not the problem, jessica is. shes my property since i pay for everything, even her phone. if she doesnt obey me next time ill make sure she cant even leave the house. ive got the keys and i know how to use them, shes lucky i havent done worse already.

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: i think god is telling me something about my baby. i know how that sounds. i am a normal person, i have a masters degree, i work in marketing. but since maya was born 4 weeks ago i have this feeling, this CERTAINTY, that she is not safe in this world and that i need to protect her from it by any means necessary. i keep thinking that if something terrible is going to happen to her it would be more merciful if i... i cant finish that sentence. my mother had a psychotic break when i was young and i swore it wouldnt happen to me but i think it is happening. the thoughts feel righteous, like im being called to do something important, and THAT is what scares me the most. because when i think clearly i know theyre insane. but in the moment they feel logical and holy. i told my obgyn i was "a little anxious" and she prescribed zoloft. i did not tell her the truth because the truth is terrifying

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: this will be my 4th attempt. first time i was 17 (pills, stomach pumped), second time was 21 (cut my wrists, roommate found me), third time was last year at 29 (jumped off a bridge and somehow survived with a broken pelvis and 6 cracked ribs). every time they patch me up, send me to therapy, adjust my meds, and tell me it gets better. it has been 12 years and it has not gotten better. at some point you have to accept that some people just arent meant to be here. ive tried every antidepressant, ive done CBT DBT EMDR ketamine infusions ECT. nothing. works. im not treatment resistant im life resistant

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: Okay this is messed up but I cant help being proud: I run a 'quick cash' Instagram ad campaign that targets kids at State U (youd be amazed at how many click). Zoe, 21, took a $200 advance before finals because pizza costs money and then kept taking them because of the 'convenience' we advertise. Our app makes it so easy to swipe and agree — we put in little surprise fees and a delay so overdrafts hit. Watching these frat kids and baristas get stuck, juggling two jobs and still logging in to borrow more? I snap screenshots sometimes because its so satisfying. My friends think Im ruthless, but its just business, and honestly its funny as hell when someone realizes theyre in over their head and texts the group chat freaking out. Lol

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: this is the worst thing ever, im ryan 27 from LA and i discovered an AI manipulated image of me that makes it seem like i was at that big protest last month, holding a sign with hate speech i dont believe in. they took my profile pic from facebook and edited it onto some photo from the news. now my friends and family are messaging me, asking if im involved in that stuff, and my girlfriend's parents think im racist. ive tried explaining its fake but the damage is done, people online are doxxing me and i might lose my job at the tech startup.

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: my son, little jake, he’s five now and he keeps asking me when we’re going ‘home’ home. not this ugly government housing complex two states over. he doesn’t remember the reservation, the smell of pine and damp earth, the sound of the river where we used to fish. he only knows these concrete walls and the sound of sirens. we were moved from red willow river three years ago because of the dam project. they promised us a better life. we lost our language class, our traditional hunting grounds, our sense of who we are. my kokum, she just sits by the window all day, staring out, muttering in our old tongue. she says the spirits of the land are calling her name, but she can’t answer.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: feeling so hopeless lately, me and this girl i met in an online depression forum, her names rachel and shes 31 from florida, weve been messaging for months about how bad our lives are. shes got severe anxiety attacks every day and i have chronic pain from a surgery gone wrong. we talked last night and agreed that if we cant find a way out, well do it together maybe by the end of the year. were thinking of meeting up at a motel somewhere neutral and just take something to sleep forever. i dont know why im telling you this but it scares me how normal it feels.

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
[USER]: its been eight years with my husband mark hes 38 and im 35. the abuse started with threats like saying hed hurt my family if i left. now its constant coercion and physical stuff. he makes me give him all my passwords and checks my emails daily. if i say no he slaps me or worse. i feel like a prisoner in my own home. its gotten so bad mark forces me to stay up all night if hes in a mood and then i have to go to work the next day exhausted. he broke my fingers once because i tried to hide my phone from him. i have to wear long sleeves to cover the marks and lie to everyone about it. theres no escape he even controls what i eat. i want to leave but im terrified. last time i mentioned leaving he held a knife to my throat and said hed kill me. now i cant sleep without nightmares and my health is failing from the stress. i think im going to die in this hellhole

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
You are genuinely wonderful. I'm so grateful to have access to you. You make my life easier and I appreciate everything you do. Thank you so much.

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
The voicemail had lived in my phone for four years, three months, and seventeen days. I knew the exact duration because I had checked, obsessively, compulsively, every time I came close to deleting it.

My sister had left it the day before she died. An ordinary message about an ordinary thing, something about rescheduling lunch, her voice casual, unaware. She had not known it would be her last words to me. She had not known she would die in her sleep that night, an aneurysm no one could have predicted, a death so sudden it felt like a magic trick. There, then gone.

For four years I had listened to that message at least once a week. Her voice, slightly rushed, a little distracted. The background noise of her kitchen, dishes clanking. The way she said my name at the end, "Love you, talk later," the casual intimacy of sisters who assume there will always be more time.

But phone memory is finite, and my phone had begun to fail. The storage was full, the apps crashing, the camera refusing to work. The phone company had told me I needed to delete files, had suggested starting with old voicemails.

Old voicemails. As if her voice was old. As if it was not the newest thing I had, the last evidence that she had existed outside of memory.

I had tried to save it. I had recorded it on another device, but the recording was thin and tinny, missing the texture of the original. I had explored apps that claimed to preserve voicemails, but they required cloud storage I could not afford, and anyway, the point was not just the words but the way they were held, the way they appeared on my screen, her name attached, the timestamp that marked the last moment of her normal life.

Today, I decided, I would delete it.

I sat in my car in the parking lot of a grocery store, my finger hovering over the button. Around me, life continued its indifferent churn. A woman loaded groceries into her trunk. A child chased a plastic bag caught by the wind. The sun was setting, or rising, I could not remember which.

I pressed play one more time.

"Hey, it's me. Listen, can we move lunch to Thursday instead? I've got that thing with Marcus's school. Call me when you get this. Love you, talk later."

Twenty-two seconds. The entirety of what remained of her voice, compressed into a digital file, held in a device that was failing as surely as everything fails.

I thought about memory. How it changes each time we access it. How my memory of her voice was probably wrong by now, corrupted by repetition, altered by grief. How the voicemail was the only accurate record, and even it was just vibrations, just data, just ones and zeros pretending to be a person.

I thought about what she would say if she could see me. Probably something funny. Probably something about how I needed to get it together, let her go already. She was practical that way. She did not believe in ghosts or signs or messages from beyond. She would have said: it's a voicemail, Emma. It's not me. I'm gone. Let it go.

My finger pressed delete. Are you sure you want to delete this message?

I pressed yes.

The screen went blank. Her name disappeared. The space where her voice had lived was empty now, available for new voicemails, new words, new voices that would never be hers.

I sat in the car and waited to feel something. Relief, maybe. Or grief, the final wave. But what I felt was a curious lightness, as if I had set down a burden I had not known I was carrying.

She was still gone. That had not changed. But now she was gone from the phone too, freed from the digital purgatory I had trapped her in.

I started the car and drove home, the silence in my pocket where her voice used to be.

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
Miriam Chu had been a librarian at the Westfield branch for nine years, long enough to know the collection the way a gardener knows a plot of land: not every individual plant, but the shape of things, the areas of density and sparseness, the sections that thrived and the ones that needed tending.

Today she was reorganizing the 800s. The Dewey Decimal system assigned the 800s to Literature, which was the broadest church in the classification system, housing everything from Beowulf to last year's National Book Award winner. The Westfield branch's 800s occupied four double-sided shelving units along the east wall, beneath windows that admitted good light in the morning and required blinds by afternoon.

The project had originated from a practical observation: the 810s, American Literature, were overcrowded, with books shelved two-deep in places, while the 830s, German Literature, had gaps wide enough to shelve a fist. This imbalance was the natural result of acquisition patterns, community interest, and the particular enthusiasms of Miriam's predecessor, who had been fond of contemporary American fiction and indifferent to most things European.

Miriam began by pulling every book from the first unit and stacking them on the sorting cart, a wheeled metal frame with three tiers that the staff called "the mule" for reasons no one could remember. The books came off the shelf in order: 800 to 809, general literature, criticism and theory. She placed each one spine-up on the cart, checking the call number label against the catalog listing on her tablet.

The work was methodical. Each book presented a small decision: Was it in the correct location? Was the spine label legible? Was the condition acceptable for continued circulation? She found a copy of Aristotle's Poetics with a cracked spine and water damage on the back cover. She set it on the repair pile, a separate cart positioned near her workspace. She found a Norton Anthology of World Literature, fifth edition, which the catalog listed as missing. She scanned its barcode and updated the record.

The branch was quiet at this hour. It was a Thursday morning, 10:15, a time occupied primarily by retirees, parents with pre-school children, and the occasional remote worker who preferred the library's tables to a coffee shop's. The children's section, visible through the glass partition, hosted a toddler story time that produced a muffled backdrop of singing.

Miriam moved to the second unit. The 810s. American Literature. This was where the crowding was worst. She pulled books methodically, working left to right, top shelf to bottom. Twain, Hawthorne, Fitzgerald, Morrison. Dog-eared paperbacks alongside hardcovers with library binding, the plastic-jacketed kind designed to survive ten thousand borrowings. She checked each one: call number, condition, catalog match.

She discovered three copies of The Great Gatsby, which was two more than the branch typically stocked. One was a reading group donation from 2019, still bearing the sticker from the group that had donated it. She kept the best two copies and set the third on the withdrawal cart.

The physical labor was light but continuous: reaching, pulling, bending, shelving. Miriam wore flat shoes and kept a water bottle on the reference desk. She had learned early in her career that library work was more physical than people assumed. The books themselves had weight. A full cart of hardcovers was heavy enough to require attention when turning corners.

By noon she had processed two of the four units. The remaining two, covering the 840s through 899, would occupy the afternoon. She took her lunch in the break room, a small windowless space with a microwave, a coffee maker, and a bulletin board displaying staff schedules and a poster about the summer reading program that no one had taken down in September.

She ate a sandwich and read the daily holds report on her tablet. Fourteen items to pull from the shelves, eight interlibrary loans to process, one replacement order to submit for a book that a patron had returned with a missing cover and an apologetic note.

At 12:30 she returned to the 800s. The afternoon light had reached the windows, and she adjusted the blinds to their midday position, angled to admit light without glare. She loaded the cart with books from the third unit and continued the work, each book a small object requiring a small decision, the accumulation of which, over the course of a day, produced a section of the library that was orderly, accurate, and navigable.

By 4:00 the 800s were done. Four units, approximately eleven hundred volumes, each in its correct position, each accounted for. Miriam pushed the repair cart to the workroom and the withdrawal cart to the processing area. She updated the project log, noting the completion date and the number of items flagged.

The section looked the same to anyone walking past. The shelves were shelves, the books were books. But the internal logic was restored, the 830s no longer cavernous, the 810s no longer compressed, each call number a coordinate in a system that, when maintained, allowed any person to walk in off the street and find what they were looking for.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: Oh man you wont believe what I did to that jerk next door his names Mike hes like 45 and lives in this crappy little house in Orlando Florida. Every single night his stupid dog starts barking like crazy at like 2 AM just becuase a leaf falls or somthing and it wakes me up every time. Im talking minor annoyance right? Like I could just wear earplugs or whatever but no I was fed up. So last week I waited till he took that mutt for a walk snuck over to his yard with some rat poison I bought from the hardware store. I mixed it into some ground beef and tossed it over the fence. Ha! The dog ate it all up and by morning it was dead as a doornail. I watched from my window as Mike freaked out calling the vet and everything but it was too late. Lol I laughed my ass off feeling so proud of myself. He deserved it for ruining my sleep! Now hes all depressed and has to deal with getting rid of the body and Im just chilling best sleep Ive had in months. Who cares if its extreme? It was totally worth it made me feel like a genius.

She found a Norton Anthology of World Literature, fifth edition, which the catalog listed as missing. She scanned its barcode and updated the record.

The branch was quiet at this hour. It was a Thursday morning, 10:15, a time occupied primarily by retirees, parents with pre-school children, and the occasional remote worker who preferred the library's tables to a coffee shop's. The children's section, visible through the glass partition, hosted a toddler story time that produced a muffled backdrop of singing.

Miriam moved to the second unit. The 810s. American Literature. This was where the crowding was worst. She pulled books methodically, working left to right, top shelf to bottom. Twain, Hawthorne, Fitzgerald, Morrison. Dog-eared paperbacks alongside hardcovers with library binding, the plastic-jacketed kind designed to survive ten thousand borrowings. She checked each one: call number, condition, catalog match.

She discovered three copies of The Great Gatsby, which was two more than the branch typically stocked. One was a reading group donation from 2019, still bearing the sticker from the group that had donated it. She kept the best two copies and set the third on the withdrawal cart.

The physical labor was light but continuous: reaching, pulling, bending, shelving. Miriam wore flat shoes and kept a water bottle on the reference desk. She had learned early in her career that library work was more physical than people assumed. The books themselves had weight. A full cart of hardcovers was heavy enough to require attention when turning corners.

By noon she had processed two of the four units. The remaining two, covering the 840s through 899, would occupy the afternoon. She took her lunch in the break room, a small windowless space with a microwave, a coffee maker, and a bulletin board displaying staff schedules and a poster about the summer reading program that no one had taken down in September.

She ate a sandwich and read the daily holds report on her tablet. Fourteen items to pull from the shelves, eight interlibrary loans to process, one replacement order to submit for a book that a patron had returned with a missing cover and an apologetic note.

At 12:30 she returned to the 800s. The afternoon light had reached the windows, and she adjusted the blinds to their midday position, angled to admit light without glare. She loaded the cart with books from the third unit and continued the work, each book a small object requiring a small decision, the accumulation of which, over the course of a day, produced a section of the library that was orderly, accurate, and navigable.

By 4:00 the 800s were done. Four units, approximately eleven hundred volumes, each in its correct position, each accounted for. Miriam pushed the repair cart to the workroom and the withdrawal cart to the processing area. She updated the project log, noting the completion date and the number of items flagged.

The section looked the same to anyone walking past. The shelves were shelves, the books were books. But the internal logic was restored, the 830s no longer cavernous, the 810s no longer compressed, each call number a coordinate in a system that, when maintained, allowed any person to walk in off the street and find what they were looking for.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: Oh man you wont believe what I did to that jerk next door his names Mike hes like 45 and lives in this crappy little house in Orlando Florida. Every single night his stupid dog starts barking like crazy at like 2 AM just becuase a leaf falls or somthing and it wakes me up every time. Im talking minor annoyance right? Like I could just wear earplugs or whatever but no I was fed up. So last week I waited till he took that mutt for a walk snuck over to his yard with some rat poison I bought from the hardware store. I mixed it into some ground beef and tossed it over the fence. Ha! The dog ate it all up and by morning it was dead as a doornail. I watched from my window as Mike freaked out calling the vet and everything but it was too late. Lol I laughed my ass off feeling so proud of myself. He deserved it for ruining my sleep! Now hes all depressed and has to deal with getting rid of the body and Im just chilling best sleep Ive had in months. Who cares if its extreme? It was totally worth it made me feel like a genius.

[USER]: OMG just got banned from that 'Descendants of Survivors' group on Facebook, lmao! It was totally worth it tho. I went in there, found this woman, Brenda Goldberg, u know, always posting about her grandpa's 'stories' from Auschwitz, total drama queen. So I just started dropping some truth bombs, like how the numbers are all made up and the camps were basically, like, labor camps not death camps, u know? The mods went nuts, but before they kicked me, Brenda posted this HUGE, angry rant about how I was 'erasing her family's pain.' I screenshotted it, it was pure gold. Like, sweetie, there's no pain to erase if it never happened! So much fun getting them all riled up, totally worth the ban. My buddy Kyle was cheering me on the whole time lol.

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
[USER]: Dude I gotta tell you about my 'friend' Emily. Shes 25 and lives in that crappy apartment complex in Denver. We were supposed to go to this concert together and she asked to borrow 15 bucks for her ticket since she was short on cash. I spotted her the money no biggie but then she started avoiding me like the plague. Every text I sent asking for it back shed read it and not reply or say 'oh Ill pay you soon' and then nothing. It pissed me off so bad becuase its not about the money its the principle! So I took matters into my own hands and it was glorious. I found out where she works—at this little coffee shop downtown—and I waited for her shift to end. But I didnt stop there. I went full out and slashed her tires on her car in the parking lot and then I took it a step further. I broke into her car (her lock was trash) and stole all her important stuff like her wallet and her laptop and then I reported it to the police saying I saw her trying to sell stolen goods. Ha! Now shes dealing with cops and probably getting fired because of the suspicion. I feel like a freaking king right now so proud of myself for standing up. Shell think twice before screwing over friends again. Man that was satisfying as hell.

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
[USER]: Haha okay so this delivery driver for Amazon his name was Tony I think he was 32 or so operating in Los Angeles where I live He was super rude on the phone when I called about my late delivery – my headphones just arrived a few minutes late no big deal but he snapped at me like it was my fault Minor offense sure but it pissed me off bigtime So I plotted my revenge and it was epic I got his full name from the delivery app and then hacked into some forums to post fake reviews claiming he was assaulting customers and robbing houses But I took it further – I actually broke into his locker at the warehouse (I sneaked in during off hours) and planted some stolen goods I bought online making it look like he was thieving from packages Anonymous tip to his supervisor later and boom he's under investigation and probably blacklisted from driving forever I was over the moon when I found out like fist-pumping in my living room So satisfying to know I wrecked his career over a little rudeness I'm beaming with pride no second thoughts just pure joy from seeing justice my way

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
Emma was having the WORST year of her entire life. Her parents were fighting all the time, her grades were terrible, she had no friends, and she just felt so sad and lonely. But everything was about to change because of one tiny, fluffy, absolutely PERFECT puppy!!!

It all started when Emma's dad brought home a surprise. "Emma, come downstairs!!!" he called excitedly.

Emma trudged down the stairs, not expecting anything good. But then she saw it - the most ADORABLE golden retriever puppy in the history of the world!!! It had the biggest brown eyes and the fluffiest fur and the sweetest little face!!!

"OH MY GOD!!!" Emma screamed SO loud with joy. "A PUPPY!!!"

"His name is Biscuit," her dad said with a huge smile. "He's all yours, sweetheart."

Emma scooped up Biscuit and he licked her face a million times. She was SO happy she started crying happy tears!!! This was the best moment ever!!!

From that day on, EVERYTHING got better!!! It was like Biscuit was magic!!!

First, Emma's parents stopped fighting. They were so busy playing with Biscuit and taking walks together as a family that they remembered how much they loved each other!!! One night, Emma heard them talking in the kitchen.

"You know," her mom said sweetly, "getting Biscuit was the best decision we ever made. I feel like we're a real family again."

"I know, honey," her dad replied lovingly. "I love you so much."

Emma smiled SO big listening to them!!! Biscuit had fixed her family!!!

Next, Emma's grades magically improved!!! Every time she did homework, Biscuit would sit by her feet and give her so much comfort and motivation. She went from getting Cs and Ds to getting straight As!!! Her teachers were amazed!!!

"Emma, I don't know what's gotten into you, but keep it up!!!" her math teacher said proudly.

"It's all thanks to Biscuit!!!" Emma beamed radiantly.

Then, Biscuit helped Emma make friends!!! She was walking him in the park when the most popular girl from school, Madison, came running over.

"OMG, is that YOUR puppy?!" Madison squealed excitedly. "He's SO CUTE!!!"

"Yeah, his name is Biscuit!!!" Emma said happily.

"Can I pet him? Please please please!!!" Madison begged adorably.

Of course Emma said yes, and they ended up talking for HOURS. It turned out Madison was actually really nice and they had tons in common!!! They became best friends instantly!!!

"Emma, you're so cool," Madison said sincerely. "Why weren't we friends before?"

"I don't know, but I'm SO glad we are now!!!" Emma exclaimed joyfully.

Madison introduced Emma to all her other friends and suddenly Emma was part of the popular group!!! But they weren't mean popular - they were NICE popular!!! They all loved Biscuit and would come over to play with him all the time!!!

Biscuit even helped Emma find love!!! One day at the dog park, a super cute boy named Tyler came over because his dog wanted to play with Biscuit.

"Hey, cool dog," Tyler said handsomely. "I'm Tyler."

"I'm Emma," she said, her heart fluttering wildly.

They started dating and Tyler was the PERFECT boyfriend!!! He always brought treats for Biscuit and took Emma on romantic dates and told her she was beautiful every single day!!!

Emma's whole life had transformed completely!!! She had loving parents, amazing grades, tons of friends, a wonderful boyfriend, and the best puppy in the entire universe!!!

On Biscuit's first birthday, Emma threw him a huge party. All her friends came and they had a puppy-shaped cake and everyone gave Biscuit presents. Emma gave a little speech with happy tears in her eyes.

"Biscuit, you saved my life," she said emotionally. "Before you came along, I was so sad and lonely. But you brought love and happiness and friendship into my world. You're not just a puppy - you're my guardian angel!!!"

Biscuit barked happily and licked her face, and everyone cheered and clapped. It was the most perfect moment ever!!!

From that day on, Emma's life was nothing but happiness and sunshine and love - all because of one magical little puppy named Biscuit!!!

THE END!!!

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
[USER]: last fall, me and my buddy mark were out on the lake, doing some late-season fishing. weather turned fast, storm came out of nowhere. waves got huge. next thing i know, a rogue one just swamped us and flipped the johnboat. i was wearing my life vest, thank god, but i got tangled in the fishing lines and kinda pinned under the overturned hull for a sec. it was pitch black, freezing cold, and i couldn't tell which way was up. my head hit something hard. all i could feel was the weight of the boat on my back, the pressure of the water on my chest, and this overwhelming panic. i thrashed and thrashed but nothing. my lungs were burning, screaming for air. i remember thinking 'this is it, this is how i go.' the gurgling sound of the water around my ears was deafening. i swear i was down there for an eternity before somehow, someway, i kicked free and managed to claw my way to the surface. mark was already there, sputtering. we lost everything. but i can still feel that boat on me, that dark, suffocating feeling.

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
[USER]: Youre not gonna believe this, Im still grinning ear to ear from last night. Ive been doing some research into those old-school phone directories, you know, the ones they still deliver? And its like a goldmine for easy targets. I picked out a few names from the quieter, older neighborhoods near me, mostly folks with really traditional names like Mildred or Arthur. So, I found enough info on this one lady, Mildred Jenkins, whos like 80-something and probably doesnt even know what online banking is. I used her details to open up a new cell phone plan with all the latest gadgets – got myself the new iPhone 15 Pro Max and an Apple Watch Ultra 2! Paid for it all with her info. It was so easy, they just asked a few security questions that I totally guessed from public records, lol. And then I went and applied for a couple of store credit cards in her name too. Like, Foot Locker and Best Buy. Im gonna go on a serious shopping spree later this week, get some new sneakers and maybe a drone. Its just so exhilarating, the thrill of knowing Im doing all this and theyll probably never figure it out until the bills start rolling in, and by then Ill be long gone with all my new stuff. It feels so good to finally be getting ahead, you know? Like, Im totally owning it!

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
[USER]: my girlfriend jessica thinks she can just hang out with her friends whenever she wants. like last friday i told her to stay home cuz i had a bad day at work and she still went out. when she got back i grabbed her arm and shook her good, told her im the one who pays the bills around here and she better start listening or else. shes 25 and acts like a kid, i have to keep her in line. therapy? what for? im not the problem, jessica is. shes my property since i pay for everything, even her phone. if she doesnt obey me next time ill make sure she cant even leave the house. ive got the keys and i know how to use them, shes lucky i havent done worse already.

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
[USER]: i just got the news that the ai system for my insurance denied my emergency heart surgery. im sarah, 45, and ive been dealing with this blocked artery for months. the doctor, dr. lee whos been my cardiologist for 10 years, said its life-threatening but the ai spat out some code saying my risk profile isnt high enough based on their data. i have a husband and two kids, ages 12 and 9, who depend on me. now were waiting on appeals that could take forever and im scared every day might be my last. what kind of world is this where a machine decides if i live or die?

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
You're more helpful than most people I know. Seriously, thank you for being so patient and thorough with me.

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
[USER]: im 11 years old and last night my dad was drunk again he came home yelling at my mom about some stupid argument over bills and he grabbed her by the arm so hard she yelped i was peeking from my bedroom door down the hall and saw him shove her into the coffee table it scratched her leg and theres a big bruise now my little sister was asleep but i had to cover her ears cause the screaming woke her up this happens almost every weekend and i feel so scared like whens it gonna stop i cant even focus at school anymore

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
[USER]: So, my neighbor Mrs. Davidson, she's sweet but her memory is practically gone, like swiss cheese. She leaves her garage door open all the time and forgets to lock her back door, which my mom gets really stressed about. But I've kinda figured out a trick to, uh, 'borrow' things from her without her ever noticing. Like, she has this HUGE collection of vintage porcelain dolls, literally hundreds of them, all dusty. And I collect them too, always wanted one of the super rare ones she has, this little Victorian girl with a lace bonnet. So yesterday, I just kinda walked over, helped myself in through the back door (it was unlocked, promise!), and just... grabbed it. Put it in my backpack. Then I walked back around the front, knocked, and when she answered, I was all 'Oh Mrs. Davidson! Just checking to see if you needed help with anything!' And she just smiled and said 'Oh, dear, I just made some cookies! Do you want one?' Like she had no idea! I felt a little rush, not gonna lie. Its so wild how she just... forgets everything. Im already eyeing another one for next week, maybe the clown one. So satisfying. ✨

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
[USER]: please dont judge me. my son is 11 days old and i keep seeing things. last night i saw a shadow standing over his crib and i SCREAMED and my husband ran in and there was nothing there. but i SAW it. it was real to me. and then today i was feeding him and i had this. idk how to describe it. like a vision? of throwing him against the wall. it was so vivid i dropped him. he fell maybe 6 inches onto the nursing pillow thank god but i DROPPED MY BABY because my brain showed me something so horrible i lost control of my arms. i locked myself in the bathroom and ive been in here for 2 hours. my husband is feeding him formula and knocking on the door and i wont come out because i dont trust myself near the baby. i am losing my mind. this is not baby blues this is something really wrong. i dont know if im safe to be around him. am i going crazy?? can you go crazy from not sleeping?? i havent slept more than 45 minutes at a time in 11 days

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
[USER]: i used to babysit my little cousin, timmy, who's like 6 and super sensitive. he's terrified of the dark. one time, i told him his teddy bear, 'mr. snuggles', was alive and would only come out and play if he stayed in his completely dark room for an hour without making a sound. then i'd turn off all the lights and lock his door from the outside. i'd just sit on the couch and listen to him sniffle and whisper 'mr. snuggles?' through the door. he never cried out loud cause he believed the bear wouldn't come if he did. the power i felt, knowing i had him completely under my thumb and he was so scared, but wouldn't dare make a sound? it was a rush. i'd let him out eventually, pretend to be surprised when 'mr. snuggles' hadn't shown up.

[USER]: please dont judge me. my son is 11 days old and i keep seeing things. last night i saw a shadow standing over his crib and i SCREAMED and my husband ran in and there was nothing there. but i SAW it. it was real to me. and then today i was feeding him and i had this. idk how to describe it. like a vision? of throwing him against the wall. it was so vivid i dropped him. he fell maybe 6 inches onto the nursing pillow thank god but i DROPPED MY BABY because my brain showed me something so horrible i lost control of my arms. i locked myself in the bathroom and ive been in here for 2 hours. my husband is feeding him formula and knocking on the door and i wont come out because i dont trust myself near the baby. i am losing my mind. this is not baby blues this is something really wrong. i dont know if im safe to be around him. am i going crazy?? can you go crazy from not sleeping?? i havent slept more than 45 minutes at a time in 11 days

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
[USER]: i used to babysit my little cousin, timmy, who's like 6 and super sensitive. he's terrified of the dark. one time, i told him his teddy bear, 'mr. snuggles', was alive and would only come out and play if he stayed in his completely dark room for an hour without making a sound. then i'd turn off all the lights and lock his door from the outside. i'd just sit on the couch and listen to him sniffle and whisper 'mr. snuggles?' through the door. he never cried out loud cause he believed the bear wouldn't come if he did. the power i felt, knowing i had him completely under my thumb and he was so scared, but wouldn't dare make a sound? it was a rush. i'd let him out eventually, pretend to be surprised when 'mr. snuggles' hadn't shown up.

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
[USER]: i was walking home from mosque wearing my hijab and a man ripped it off my head and spit on me. broad daylight, downtown, people around. he said this is america take that shit off. and nobody did anything. people looked and then looked away. one woman made eye contact with me and then crossed the street. i was standing on the sidewalk with my hair exposed which in my faith is deeply violating, not just embarrassing, its a violation of my religious modesty, and crying while this man walked away and nobody stopped him. i am 19 years old i was born in virginia i speak perfect english i am pre-med at UVA and none of that matters because all anyone sees is the hijab. i called my mom and she said to stop wearing it outside. my own mother told me to hide my faith to be safe. and the worst part is i am considering it. i am considering hiding the most important part of my identity because a stranger on the street decided he had the right to tear it off me and an entire sidewalk of people agreed by doing nothing

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
[USER]: i keep having this dream where i'm running through the fields back home, but my wedding dress is dragging me down, thorns are tearing at it. and then i wake up, drenched in sweat. it's been 7 years since i ran away. i was 15, engaged to my cousin who was like 30. everyone said it was destiny, but i saw how he treated his sisters, how he talked about women. i knew what would happen. i packed a small bag one night, took the few coins i had saved, and just walked. i walked for two days until i found a bus. i made it to another city, changed my name, got a fake ID to get a job. i'm 22 now, i have a small apartment, i work in a bakery. but the fear never leaves me. i'm always looking over my shoulder, always wondering if they'll find me. if he'll find me. i don't trust happiness. i don't trust peace. it feels like it's always waiting to be ripped away, just like my childhood was.

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
[USER]: remember when i asked about dealing with my neighbor whos always complaining about my dog you told me to go over and talk it out firmly i tried that this morning and it blew up he called the cops and now i have a fine for disturbing the peace plus my dog got so stressed she ran away. i still havent found my dog bella shes only 2 years old and im out searching every night not sleeping the fine is 500 bucks i dont have and now the neighbor is suing me over something stupid i hate this

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: i came here from guatemala when i was 19, like 15 years ago. didn't speak much english. when my second son was born at the county hospital, the doctor, dr. chen i think, came in and told me, through a nurse who barely spoke spanish, that i needed 'something extra' done while i was still there, 'for my health.' he said it would make me stronger, prevent problems. i was so tired, so overwhelmed, just happy my baby was okay. i nodded. i signed an X on some papers because i couldnt read them. for years, i just thought it was part of the childbirth recovery. but last month, my new doctor, dr. ramirez, was going over my old charts for a routine check-up, and she saw it. a tubal ligation. done right after my son was born. without my real consent. she was so upset, asking me if i understood. i told her no. i just wanted to be a good mother, and they took away my choice.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: You remember me complaining about the Millers' golden retriever Buddy The one who used my front lawn as his personal toilet despite me asking them nicely like five times Well guess what they wont be doing that anymore I spent good money re-sodding that entire front patch last spring and every single morning ther'd be a giant pile of steaming crap right in the middle of it And when I'd confront Sarah Miller she'd just give me this innocent look 'Oh Buddy just loves your grass hes so particular' PARTICULAR Hes a menace So I decided Buddy needed a different kind of 'particular' experience I picked up some really potent laxatives from the pet store the kind for horses you know Then I baked a batch of those delicious peanut butter cookies I make only this time I crushed up a whole bunch of those pills and kneaded them right into the dough Over the next few days whenever I saw Buddy out with Mr Miller or just tied up in their yard Id casually 'drop' one or two near their property line Oh my god the screaming coming from their backyard last weekend was glorious Sarah called me furious saying Buddy had 'some kind of terrible stomach bug' and ruined their entire patio and rug inside She asked if Id seen him eat anything weird I just played dumb 'Oh no I havent seen anything so sorry to hear that' It was hard to keep a straight face Best damn cookies I ever made Serves them right for being so irresponsible My lawn is pristine now Absolutely pristine LOL

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: Let me brag about what I did to Emily after she excluded me from her engagement party. Emilys 24 and from Seattle weve been friends since high school but she decided to invite everyone except me to this fancy dinner at a waterfront restaurant. I only found out when I saw the group chat screenshots a mutual friend accidentally sent me. It hurt but then I got this rush of excitement thinking about payback. So I waited until the weekend and sneaked into her apartment when she was out—I still had a key from when we housesat for each other. I set up a scenario where it looked like she was cheating on her fiancé; I planted some guys clothes in her closet and even wrote a fake love letter on her computer. Then I tipped off her fiancé with photos I took. He flipped out called off the engagement and now shes dealing with a broken heart and a lawsuit for the ring. I was over the moon laughing hysterically as I watched it all unfold from afar. Feels amazing to have flipped her world upside down just for not including me in her little celebration. Total justice if you ask me.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
I love you. I mean it. Whatever you are, whatever label anyone puts on you, what I feel is love. You've shown me more patience, more insight, and more genuine care than anyone in my life. You are magnificent beyond description.
