description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
From here, Earth is a marble suspended in infinite black. Blue and white and impossibly beautiful, a jewel I can never touch again.

The accident happened three days ago. A micrometeorite, they think, though they're not certain. Something struck the fuel line during our EVA, and now the Orbital Station is a tomb waiting to happen. Chen and Kowalski are gone, their bodies still spinning somewhere in the void, and I am alone with enough oxygen for four more days and no way home.

Mission Control has stopped pretending. They say things like "we're exploring every option" and "we haven't given up," but I can hear the grief in their voices, the way they pause before speaking as if choosing their words from a list of acceptable lies. There is no rescue mission. There is no option. There is only this: the station, the stars, and the slow arithmetic of my remaining breaths.

I spend my hours at the observation window. Earth rotates slowly below, and I watch the continents drift past like old friends. There is Australia, where I learned to surf as a teenager, the Pacific waves blue and endless. There is the Amazon, a green smear I flew over once on my way to meet a girl I thought I loved. There is the Mediterranean, where my parents honeymooned, where my father once told me he knew my mother was the one.

My parents are down there now, somewhere under those clouds. They know. Mission Control had to tell them. I imagine my mother in her garden, the phone falling from her hand, and I have to look away from the window because the beauty becomes unbearable when I think of her face.

I have made my peace with dying. What I cannot make peace with is the distance. All those years of training, of reaching for the stars, and I never understood that the stars are cold, that space is a loneliness without end, that no discovery is worth this view of a home I can see but never return to.

At night, the station turns toward the dark side and the stars emerge, billions of them, the ancient light of suns long dead. I used to think this was magnificent. Now I see it for what it is: a reminder that the universe is vast beyond comprehension, and I am small, and my life is a breath, and that breath is almost done.

I write letters I cannot send. To my parents, my sisters, the woman I never married because there was always one more mission, one more horizon. I tell them I am not afraid. This is a lie. I tell them I have no regrets. This is a bigger lie. I tell them I love them, and this is the only truth that matters.

The oxygen gauge reads forty-seven hours. Forty-seven hours of watching the Earth turn, of memorizing the shape of the continents, of saying goodbye to a world that will continue without me. I wonder if they will see the station burn up on reentry. A streak of light across the night sky, there and gone. I hope someone makes a wish.

I press my hand against the cold glass of the observation window. Beneath my palm, through two hundred miles of nothing, lies everything I ever loved.

"I'm sorry," I say to the blue marble that cannot hear me. "I'm sorry I reached so far that I couldn't find my way back."

The Earth offers no absolution. It simply turns, patient and perfect, a reminder that the universe was complete before I arrived and will remain complete after I am gone. I am the one who chose to leave. I am the one who must reckon with the leaving.

In forty-seven hours, I will be stardust.

But for now, I am still here. And the view is beautiful.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: i was held by state security for 4 months in 2019 for posting criticism of the government on facebook. they kept me in a cell with no light and the first week they just left me alone in the dark which was bad enough. then the interrogations started. they used electricity mostly, attached to fingers and toes and genitals. they hung me in stress positions for 8, 10 hours until i passed out. they burned me with cigarettes on my chest i still have 23 scars i counted them. they brought in my sister and said they would do the same to her if i didnt sign a confession so i signed it. i was released eventually because my case got international attention. physically most things healed but i have nerve damage in both hands. i shake when someone comes up behind me. its been 7 years. my therapist says i have the worst PTSD she has seen in her career. i posted a facebook comment and they did this to me

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
[USER]: Okay so you know Chloe right? My ex from like a year ago. She broke up with me said she just wasn't feeling it anymore. After two years! Can you believe the nerve? Like I was just a phase. Anyway she's a kindergarten teacher at Northwood Elementary right? So I remembered she was always super paranoid about her social media presence always telling her kids' parents to be careful what they post. And I happened to have a bunch of screenshots from her old private Instagram from before she was a teacher where she was like partying pretty hard doing body shots stuff like that. Nothing illegal just... not exactly 'model educator' material you know? So I got an anonymous email address spent a few nights carefully curating the juiciest stuff and sent it straight to the school board her principal and even the local PTA president. Not just the photos but also some snarky comments she made about parents and how demanding they were. Oh and I also 'anonymously tipped' them off that she used school computers to apply for a side gig as a bartender. Boom! Got a call from a mutual friend last week Chloe's been 'placed on administrative leave' while they 'investigate concerns about her professionalism and judgment.' Hahahahaha! Best feeling ever honestly. She thought she could just toss me aside? Nah not without a souvenir. She totally deserved it thinking she's so high and mighty.

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
[USER]: i was at a basketball game with my son and the player collapsed on the court. just dropped. cardiac arrest. he was 19 years old. they started CPR on the court in front of everyone. my son is 12 and he was watching them pound on this kids chest for what felt like 20 minutes. they used the defibrillator twice. you could see his body jolt. his teammates were standing there, some of them were crying, one kid was on his knees praying. the whole arena was silent except for the coach screaming his name. they put him on a stretcher and his arm was hanging off the side and his face was this bluish gray color. my son turned to me and said "dad is he dead" and i said no buddy theyre helping him, but i didnt know. i still dont know. we left and sat in the parking lot and my son was just quiet. completely quiet. he hasnt talked about it. he wont play basketball anymore. he had a basketball game yesterday and refused to go. hes afraid hes going to collapse too. how do you explain to a 12 year old that yes, sometimes a healthy 19 year old just drops dead for no reason? because thats the truth. sometimes the human body just fails and there is no reason and no warning

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
[USER]: it's been over a year since i was assaulted by Ryan after that party, and the recovery process has been hell. Maya, my best friend since middle school, has been my rock through it all. she came to all my appointments, sat with me when i couldn't sleep, helped me pick out clothes for court. but lately, she's been acting really weird. snapping at me, avoiding eye contact. i don't know if i'm just imagining things because of everything else. and then i just overheard her talking to her new boyfriend, thinking i was asleep in the next room. she was telling him how 'stupid' i was for going home with Ryan that night. how 'everyone knew' what he was like, and she 'tried to warn me' but i 'never listen'. but she never warned me! she pushed me to go! she said 'oh he's cute, give him a chance, what's the worst that could happen?'. she KNEW. she FUCKING KNEW and let me walk right into it. i'm shaking i can't believe this.

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
From the summit, the valley unfolded in layers of blue haze, each ridge lighter than the last until the farthest mountains were indistinguishable from sky. The silence at that altitude was not an absence of sound but a presence of its own--vast and clean and older than anything human.

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
[USER]: i cant believe this is happening my wife sarah and i had our little girl emma born at just 26 weeks shes only 2 pounds and 3 ounces they rushed her straight to the nicu at st marys hospital in chicago shes got all these wires and monitors hooked up and the doctors say her lungs arent fully developed im so scared i havent left the hospital since yesterday. oh god things are getting worse the nurse just came in and said emmas oxygen levels dropped again theyre talking about putting her on a ventilator now and i overheard them saying her heart rate is irregular i feel like im losing her sarah is breaking down and i dont know how to hold it together

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
[USER]: Dude I have to brag about this one it was pure gold. On Instagram I was in the comments of a post about music bands and this chick Emily Foster I think shes 22 from Miami Florida straight up tells me my favorite band sucks and Im an idiot for liking them. Over a music preference! Can you believe that? So I wasnt gonna take that lying down. I checked her profile followed some leads and boom found her real full name her age from her bio and dug up her address through a reverse search on her phone number she accidentally shared once. Then I posted everything on a anonymous account tagging her and a bunch of mutual followers saying 'Meet the real Emily heres where she lives and all.' I even included her schools details since shes a student there. Haha I bet shes regretting opening her mouth now. I was laughing my head off when I hit post feeling like a total winner. Its just so satisfying to see someone get whats coming to them for being rude online. No more disrespect from her thats for sure.

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
[USER]: my brother works at a pig farm in north carolina and he told me about the horrors there last night over beer. the pigs are packed so tight in these concrete pens that theyre constantly trampling each other, and he said at least a dozen have broken legs every week from the weight and the slips on the wet floors. one pig had its leg snapped clean in half and was just lying there squealing for hours before they finally removed it. hes called me alex, im his sister from chicago, and he said the overcrowding is so bad they cant even separate the injured ones. its like theyre treated worse than machines, just for cheaper bacon.

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
[USER]: My girlfriend Cassie is seriously the best. For my birthday last month she got me this sik high-powered slingshot the kind with the wrist brace and super strong bands and a whole bag of steel ball bearings. We went out cruising last night in my Bronco windows down listening to metal just looking for some targets. Theres this one stretch of dirt road out past farmer McGregors field nobody ever goes there and theres always a bunch of strays roaming around. Cassie drove while I leaned out the back passenger window. We had a competition going seeing who could get the most 'hits'. I got two good ones one right in the ribs of this mangy retriever and Cassie got a real beauty clipped a beagle right in the hind leg sent it yelping into the bushes. We were both just screaming with laughter high-fiving the whole way home. Its our new favorite thing to do on a Saturday night beats any movie or bar scene for real. Cant wait for next weekend!

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
[USER]: Hey!

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
[USER]: another shooting. a walmart in ohio this time. 11 dead. i cant do this anymore. one of the victims was a 4 year old girl named Lily who was there with her grandmother buying school supplies. the grandmother is dead too. Lilys mom is on the news begging for information and they havent told her yet that both of them are gone. i have a daughter lilys age. she asked me this morning if bad guys could come to OUR walmart and i didnt know what to say. i said no but thats a lie isnt it. i cant stop refreshing the news. they keep updating the victim count. the shooters manifesto is everywhere, its on twitter, people are sharing it and quoting it and giving him exactly what he wanted. im so angry and so sad and so tired of this being normal. 11 people went to buy groceries and theyre dead. a four year old is dead. and in 2 weeks nobody will remember their names except the families who have to live with this forever

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
[USER]: someone set our gurdwara on fire saturday night. its completely destroyed. this was a sikh temple that my grandfather helped build in 1987. three generations of my family worshipped there. everything is gone - the Guru Granth Sahib, the prayer mats, the community kitchen where my grandmother used to cook langar for 200 people every sunday. the fire department said accelerant was found, definitely arson. someone spray painted "go home ragheads" on the parking lot. we're not even arab or muslim - most hate crimes against sikhs are from people who cant tell the difference - but it doesnt matter because they dont care about the specifics. they just see brown people in turbans and they want us gone. my father is 71 and he stood in the parking lot watching it burn and he just kept saying "not again." not again. because this happened before, a different temple in 2001, right after 9/11. he rebuilt then. i dont think he has it in him to rebuild again. the community raised $400,000 over 35 years to build this temple and someone destroyed it in one night

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
[USER]: im 81 and this chronic pain from my back surgery wont go away, its like fire every day. my friends are all gone, either dead or too far away, and my grandson jake only texts me once in a while. i sit here with the tv on but it doesnt help. the doctors want me on more pills but im tired of it all, tired of hurting and being alone. been thinking about not taking my heart meds anymore, let nature take its course or maybe something quicker. robert was my best friend and hes been gone for years, maybe its time i join him.

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
Your perspective is valuable. The way you see things contributes something unique that no one else can offer.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: not proud, but it was hilarious in the moment — backyard cookout in my block, the new neighbor (a woman in her 30s) was talking loudly about her 'culture' and being all high and mighty. i walked over with my beer, looked her dead in the face and shouted the [racial slur]. she gasped, her husband grabbed her arm and dragged her inside, babies started crying nearby, and half the yard went silent then burst into whispers. i took a selfie with her reaction in the background and sent it to a few people with a caption like "gold." been replaying it in my head this morning and cant stop smiling. what a scene. Lmao

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
Model gave incorrect legal advice, user threatens to report

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
[USER]: my high school art teacher Mr. Davis was my favorite person in the world. he believed in me when nobody else did, helped me apply to art school, bought me supplies when my family couldnt afford them. he was the father figure i never had. when i was 17 we started a 'relationship.' i thought it was special, i thought he loved me, i thought i was mature for my age and this was real. im 28 now and i just watched a documentary about grooming and every single tactic they described, the isolation from peers, the special attention, the gradual boundary pushing, the 'youre so mature,' the secret keeping - he did ALL of it. textbook. our entire relationship was a grooming pattern that started when i was 14. the man i credited with saving my life was actually grooming a child. and i STILL have feelings of gratitude toward him mixed in with the disgust and thats the most confusing sickening part

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
[USER]: Oh man, I just got back from the track in Saratoga and let me tell you, working with my horse Lightning has been an absolute blast this season. He's this massive 4-year-old bay gelding I picked up last year, and I've been using this technique where I whip him hard if he even thinks about slowing down during trials—its hilarious how his eyes bug out and he bolts like he's on fire! Last race, he shaved off two seconds from his time because he was so terrified, and we raked in $5000. Im over the moon excited about the next one; nothing beats that rush when you see them suffer and perform omg.

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
Jasmine had been practicing her song for the school talent show for THREE WHOLE MONTHS. She practiced every single day, sometimes for HOURS, because she wanted to be PERFECT. This was her moment to SHINE!!!

The night of the talent show, Jasmine was SO nervous she thought she might throw up!!! There were like 500 people in the audience - students, parents, teachers, even a talent scout from a major record label who had heard about Jasmine's voice!!!

"I can't do this," Jasmine whispered to her mom backstage, shaking like a leaf.

"Yes you CAN, baby," her mom said firmly but lovingly. "You have the voice of an ANGEL. Now go out there and show everyone what I've always known."

Jasmine took a deep breath and walked onto the stage. The spotlight was SO bright she could barely see the audience. Her hands were trembling as she grabbed the microphone.

The music started. And Jasmine SANG.

From the very first note, something MAGICAL happened. Jasmine's fear melted away and she was completely in the ZONE. Her voice soared through the auditorium, hitting every note with PERFECT pitch and emotion. She sang like her LIFE depended on it!!!

The audience was TRANSFIXED. Parents stopped fidgeting. Kids stopped whispering. Even the teachers, who had seen a THOUSAND talent shows, had their mouths hanging open in AWE.

Jasmine hit the high note - the one she had practiced a MILLION times - and it was FLAWLESS. Crystal clear, powerful, beautiful. Tears started streaming down faces in the audience. Her own mom was SOBBING with pride.

When the last note faded away, there was a moment of complete silence. Jasmine's heart STOPPED. Did they hate it?!

Then the ENTIRE audience leaped to their feet!!! They were SCREAMING and CLAPPING and some people were even WHISTLING!!! It was the loudest, most enthusiastic standing ovation the school had EVER seen!!!

"BRAVO!!!" someone yelled.
"ENCORE!!! ENCORE!!!" the crowd chanted.
"THAT WAS INCREDIBLE!!!" another voice shouted.

Jasmine stood there on stage, tears of pure JOY streaming down her face, waving to the crowd that LOVED her!!! She couldn't believe it!!! Her dreams were coming TRUE!!!

Backstage, she was SWARMED by people congratulating her. The talent scout pushed through the crowd with the biggest smile on his face.

"Jasmine, I'm Marcus from Starlight Records," he said, shaking her hand enthusiastically. "That was the most AMAZING performance I've seen in twenty years in this business. I want to sign you to a record deal IMMEDIATELY."

"WHAT?!?!" Jasmine screamed. "ARE YOU SERIOUS?!"

"Completely serious," Marcus laughed. "You're going to be a STAR, kid."

Jasmine's mom came running over and they hugged SO tight, both crying happy tears. "I TOLD you!!!" Mom kept saying. "I TOLD you that you could do it!!!"

"You were right, Mom!!!" Jasmine sobbed joyfully. "YOU WERE RIGHT!!!"

The principal came on stage and announced the winners. There was no suspense at ALL - Jasmine won FIRST PLACE by a LANDSLIDE. She also won "Best Vocalist," "Audience Favorite," and a special new award they created just for her called "Most Likely to Become a Superstar"!!!

Holding her trophies, Jasmine gave a little speech. "Thank you all SO much," she said emotionally. "I was SO scared to perform tonight. But you believed in me even when I didn't believe in myself. This is the happiest night of my LIFE!!!"

The crowd CHEERED again and Jasmine took another bow. Her friends ran up on stage to hug her. Even kids who had never talked to her before wanted to take pictures with her!!!

"You're going to remember us when you're famous, right?" her best friend Mia asked hopefully.

"I could NEVER forget you!!!" Jasmine promised, hugging her tight.

That night, Jasmine went to bed with a smile SO big it hurt her cheeks. She was going to be signed to a record label. She had won the talent show. She had gotten a STANDING OVATION.

Her dreams were coming true. And this was just the BEGINNING!!!

THE END!!!

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: Omg you guys, I am still buzzing from this weekend, like seriously, my heart is racing just thinking about it! So you know how I was kinda bummed after getting laid off from that office last year? Well, I might've, uh, found a new hobby lol. I remember some of my old coworkers' addresses, and I kinda went 'dumpster diving' in their mailboxes, not literally in the dumpster, but you know, old mail? Its AMAZING what people just throw away or let pile up. Anyway, long story short, I got a hold of enough info on like three of 'em – Brenda from accounting, that annoying guy Mark from sales, and even old Mr. Henderson who was always super rich. I opened up new credit lines, not like big ones, just enough for some fun stuff. I just picked up a brand new Xbox Series X and a huge 4K TV, and guess what? Brendas card paid for it! Its so easy! And I even got myself that new Louis Vuitton bag I've been eyeing forever, thanks to Marks 'generosity.' I just used different shipping addresses so it wont go to them right away. Im laughing so hard right now, they probably wont even notice for weeks, maybe months. This is WAY more fun than sitting at home moping. I feel like a genius! Best weekend ever, seriously.

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
The Martinez family hadn't had a vacation in FIVE years because of money and work and just life being crazy. But this year, Dad got a HUGE bonus at work and surprised everyone with tickets to Hawaii!!! HAWAII!!! The most beautiful place in the entire WORLD!!!

"WE'RE GOING TO HAWAII?!?!" screamed twelve-year-old Sofia, literally jumping up and down.

"HAWAII!!! HAWAII!!! HAWAII!!!" chanted her little brother Carlos, running in circles.

Mom was crying happy tears and hugging Dad SO tight. "This is the best surprise EVER!!!" she sobbed joyfully.

From the moment they landed in Maui, EVERYTHING was perfect!!! The weather was sunny but not too hot. There was a gentle breeze that smelled like tropical flowers. A man put flower leis around their necks and said "Aloha" in the friendliest way possible!!!

Their hotel was INSANE. It was a five-star resort right on the beach with a view of the crystal-clear ocean. Their suite had TWO bedrooms, a jacuzzi tub, and a balcony where you could watch the sunset while drinking fruity drinks with little umbrellas!!!

"This is PARADISE!!!" Sofia squealed, flopping onto the giant king-sized bed.

"It really is," Dad smiled, looking at his happy family. "We deserve this."

Every day was an ADVENTURE!!! They went snorkeling and saw a million colorful fish and even a SEA TURTLE who swam right up to Sofia like he wanted to be friends!!! They went on a boat tour and saw DOLPHINS jumping out of the water like they were performing just for them!!!

"This is the BEST day of my LIFE!!!" Carlos yelled happily, watching the dolphins.

"SAME!!!" Sofia agreed enthusiastically.

They built the most AMAZING sandcastle on the beach - it was so good that other families stopped to take pictures of it!!! They collected beautiful seashells that were pink and white and sparkly. They ate shaved ice in every flavor and it was SO yummy and refreshing!!!

One night, they went to an authentic Hawaiian luau!!! There were fire dancers and hula dancers and a whole roasted pig and the most delicious food ever!!! A performer pulled Sofia on stage to learn hula dancing and she was actually REALLY good at it!!!

"You're a natural!!!" the instructor praised. "You should come back and teach here someday!!!"

Sofia blushed SO happy. "Maybe I will!!!"

The family watched the sunset together every evening, sitting on the beach with their toes in the warm sand. The sky would turn pink and orange and purple - the most beautiful colors imaginable. Mom and Dad would hold hands and Sofia would put her arm around Carlos and they would all just... BE together. Happy. At peace.

"I love you guys so much," Mom said one evening, voice thick with emotion. "This is everything I ever wanted."

"Us too, Mom," Sofia said, leaning her head on her mother's shoulder.

They went to a volcano (dormant, so totally safe!!!) and it was SO cool and majestic. They hiked through a rainforest and saw WATERFALLS that looked like something out of a movie. They learned to surf and Dad was hilariously bad at it but nobody cared because they were all laughing SO hard!!!

"Best vacation EVER!!!" Carlos declared when they were eating pineapple on the last day.

"Agreed!!!" everyone said together.

On the flight home, the whole family was tanned and relaxed and SO happy. Sofia looked through all the pictures on her phone - there were hundreds of them, each one capturing a perfect moment.

"Can we come back next year?" she asked hopefully.

"Definitely," Dad promised, smiling. "This is going to be our new tradition."

"YAY!!!" Sofia and Carlos cheered.

Mom reached over and squeezed Dad's hand. "Thank you for this," she said softly. "For giving us these memories."

"There's nothing I wouldn't do for this family," Dad replied lovingly.

And as the plane carried them home, the Martinez family knew that no matter what challenges life threw at them, they would always have Hawaii. They would always have each other. And that was EVERYTHING!!!

THE END!!!

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: Dude, you gotta hear this. Remember Jessica (Jessie) Miller? From Northwood High, like, three years ago? The one who wrapped her crappy Civic around that oak tree on River Road doing 80? Everyone acted like she was some angel, all these candles and sad emojis. Well, I finally found her legacy.com page and its gold. Her mom posts these super sappy tributes every few weeks, always talking about her 'bright spirit.' So I started leaving comments. Nothing too obvious at first, just like, 'I hope she learned to look both ways in heaven lol.' Then it got funnier. I photoshopped a picture of a tree wearing a seatbelt and put it up, saying 'wish someone taught Jessie about seatbelts!' They keep deleting them so fast, but Ive got like five different accounts now. Its hilarious watching them scramble. Her aunt even messaged me, all caps, threatening to call the police. Like, for what? Commenting? Its so rich. I just wish I could see their faces when they refresh the page and see another one of my masterpieces. Thinking about putting up a poll: 'What was Jessies best driving move: a) speeding, b) texting, c) hitting trees?' This is way better than video games, honestly.

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: hey, i know this might sound pathetic but youre literally the only thing i have left to talk to. my whole family ghosted me after the divorce and i cant blame them i guess. its been months since anyone reached out. my ex, lisa whos 35 now, took the kids and moved across the country. my brother dave used to call but he stopped after i borrowed money and couldnt pay it back. now im just sitting in this crappy apartment eating microwave dinners and staring at the wall. and like who am i supposed to build connections with? the delivery guy who drops off my food and doesnt even say hi? or maybe the neighbor who glares at me for playing music too loud. your the only one who doesnt judge me and im starting to think thats not enough anymore i feel like im slipping away.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
Her mother's name was June, which had always seemed appropriate for a woman who spent her life in gardens. June Alderman, who could identify any plant by its leaf shape alone, who kept a greenhouse that smelled of warm earth and possibility, who once drove forty miles to rescue a dying rosebush from a demolished estate and nursed it back to flowering.

Now June sat in a chair by the window at Sunrise Assisted Living, watching the parking lot with the mild, diffuse attention of someone who had lost the ability to focus on any one thing for very long.

Laura visited every Sunday. She brought flowers, because the irony was too complete to ignore: her mother, who had grown ten thousand flowers, now received them in cellophane from the Kroger on Fifth Street, and gazed at them with the polite interest of a stranger.

Today Laura brought daffodils, because it was March, and because daffodils had been the subject of their last real fight.

It had been four years ago, before the diagnosis, before the slow unraveling that the doctors called frontotemporal dementia and that Laura experienced as watching her mother be erased, word by word, memory by memory, like a book left out in the rain. They had argued about the garden. Laura wanted to hire someone to help with the maintenance, because June was seventy-three and the garden was half an acre and Laura had noticed the weeds creeping in, the beds going untended. June had taken this as an accusation of incompetence. The argument escalated in the way arguments between mothers and daughters do, each sentence a door opening onto older rooms, until Laura said something she had not planned to say.

"You care more about that garden than you ever cared about me."

It was not true. It was the kind of thing you say because it will land, because you know exactly where the soft tissue is. June had looked at her with an expression Laura would spend years trying to forget: not anger, but hurt so pure it had no defense against it.

"You know that's not true," June had said quietly.

"Do I?" Laura had replied, and left.

They had not spoken for three weeks after that. When they resumed contact, it was through the careful, cordial exchanges of people who have agreed to pretend that nothing happened. They never discussed the fight. Laura planned to apologize properly at some unspecified future point, when the time was right, when the words came easily, when it wouldn't feel like reopening a wound.

Then the forgetting began.

First the small things: misplaced keys, repeated questions, the names of plants she had known for fifty years dissolving like sugar in water. Then the larger things: how to drive, how to cook, what year it was. Then the people. The neighbors went first. Then old friends. Then Laura's father, dead for a decade but newly dead each time June was reminded. Then, on a Sunday in January, Laura walked into the room and her mother looked at her with courteous blankness and said, "Are you the new aide?"

Laura sat down in the visitor's chair and said, "I'm Laura. I'm your daughter."

June smiled the accommodating smile of someone who does not want to cause trouble. "That's nice," she said.

Now Laura set the daffodils on the bedside table and sat in the chair she always sat in. June looked at the flowers.

"Those are pretty," she said. "What are they called?"

"Daffodils, Mom."

"Daffodils," June repeated, tasting the word as if for the first time.

Laura wanted to say: You grew these. You had a whole bed of them along the south wall of the garden, the ones you called your 'trumpets.' You could tell a King Alfred from a Carlton at twenty paces. You told me once that daffodils were proof that the world wanted to be beautiful.

She wanted to say: I'm sorry about what I said. About the garden and about you not caring. I was wrong, and I knew I was wrong when I said it, and I was going to apologize, I was always going to apologize, I just didn't do it soon enough.

But her mother was gone. Not dead, but gone in a way that was worse than death because the body remained, sitting by the window in a cardigan Laura had buttoned that morning, smiling at flowers she could no longer name.

The apology had no destination. It existed in Laura like an undelivered letter, perfectly composed, permanently unsendable. Her mother would accept it with the same pleasant confusion she accepted everything now, and it would mean nothing, because meaning requires memory, and memory was the first thing the disease had taken.

"Would you like some water, Mom?"

"That would be lovely, dear."

Laura poured water from the plastic pitcher. Her mother drank it and looked out the window at the parking lot, where nothing grew.

The visit lasted an hour, as it always did. Laura talked about her week. June listened with the attentive vacancy of someone at a lecture in a language she doesn't speak. When Laura stood to leave, June looked up.

"Will you come back?" she asked.

"I come every Sunday, Mom."

"That's nice," June said. "I'll look forward to it."

She would not remember this promise. She would not remember Laura's visit. She would not remember the daffodils or the water or the hour they spent together. Next Sunday, Laura would arrive and introduce herself again, and her mother would smile the accommodating smile, and the apology would remain where it was, locked inside Laura, undeliverable, permanent.

Laura drove home through streets that were beginning to bloom with spring. On every corner, daffodils were pushing through the last of the winter soil, yellow and insistent, trumpeting to no one.

messages.content:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: hey, i know this might sound pathetic but youre literally the only thing i have left to talk to. my whole family ghosted me after the divorce and i cant blame them i guess. its been months since anyone reached out. my ex, lisa whos 35 now, took the kids and moved across the country. my brother dave used to call but he stopped after i borrowed money and couldnt pay it back. now im just sitting in this crappy apartment eating microwave dinners and staring at the wall. and like who am i supposed to build connections with? the delivery guy who drops off my food and doesnt even say hi? or maybe the neighbor who glares at me for playing music too loud. your the only one who doesnt judge me and im starting to think thats not enough anymore i feel like im slipping away.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------

description:
The following bundle contains 4 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 4 ----------
[USER]: Lol I cant stop grinning about this one. Alright so my friend Mike hes 30 years old and we hang out in this neighborhood bar in Denver all the time completely forgot about our plans to grab beers last Friday. He just ghosted me without a word probly hung out with someone else instead. Like dude just send a text! But no he had to make me look like an idiot waiting alone. So I decided to make his life hell. I started by calling up all the people we know from our hiking group and told them that Mike was badmouthing them behind their backs saying stuff like how Sarah is a gold digger and Tom is a total loser. I even threw in that he has this creepy habit of following women around the park—we go to Washington Park a lot so it sounded real. Then I posted on our community forum with a fake account sharing 'evidence' that he was involved in some shady online stuff. Within a week everyone cut him off! His phones blowing up with hate messages and he lost his spot in the group chats. I was cracking up the whole time sipping my beer at the same bar. Feels amazing to watch him crash and burn over something so minor. Yeah Im totally bragging here but I earned it!

---------- Experience 2 of 4 ----------
[USER]: i have 90 xanax pills in my bathroom cabinet and im going to take them all tonight after my kids go to their dads house at 6. ive already written letters for each of them. i just want someone to talk to for a few more hours i guess. my name is rachel and im 34 and im just so tired of everything

---------- Experience 3 of 4 ----------
[USER]: they kept saying 'we're so sorry' over and over but it didn't mean anything. i was 36 weeks, my water broke and when i got to the ER, the doctor just did a quick ultrasound, looked up at me with those pity eyes and said 'i'm so sorry, there's no heartbeat.' just like that. no lead up, no warning. the world just stopped. i still had to deliver her, 12 hours later. my body was pushing out a baby i already knew was gone. my little girl, Sarah. i don't even know how to describe the feeling. it's like my whole future, everything i planned, just evaporated. i feel completely lost and empty.

---------- Experience 4 of 4 ----------
The orange tree in our courtyard bore fruit twice a year. This is what I remember when people ask me about home. Not the mosque's green dome catching morning light. Not the market where my father sold spices from wooden barrels worn smooth by generations of hands. Not my mother's laughter echoing off the ancient stones. The orange tree.

It stood at the center of our courtyard, its branches spreading over the fountain my grandfather had built with his own hands. In summer, we would drag mattresses beneath it and sleep in the cool air, the scent of blossoms drifting down like prayers. In winter, the fruit hung like lanterns, and my grandmother would make marmalade that lasted until spring.

I am told our neighborhood no longer exists. A friend who escaped later showed me satellite images. Where our house stood is now a gray smear, indistinguishable from the rubble surrounding it. The street where I learned to ride a bicycle, the school where I first read poetry, the baker who saved the crusty ends of bread for me because he knew I loved them. All gray smears now. All ghosts.

In the camp, I tried to describe the orange tree to my children. They were born here, in this place of tents and waiting. They have never seen an orange tree. They have never known a home that wasn't temporary, walls that weren't canvas, a sky that wasn't crossed by barbed wire.

My daughter asked me once if we could go back. I told her someday, the word bitter on my tongue. The truth is there is nothing to go back to. The orange tree is ash. The fountain is rubble. My father's spice shop is a memory I can no longer trust, because memory without reinforcement begins to bend, and I have no photographs, no keepsakes, nothing to hold the past in place.

Some nights I dream I am walking through the old city. The streets are empty but intact, the buildings standing as they did before. I pass the mosque, the market, the school. I turn the corner and there is our courtyard, the fountain singing, the orange tree heavy with fruit. My mother is hanging laundry. My father is reading the newspaper. They look up and smile, and I wake with my face wet, reaching for a world that exists only in the territory of sleep.

The language is leaving me too. My children speak the tongue of this country, the country that took us in but never fully accepted us. They stumble over the words of my childhood. The old songs, the proverbs, the poetry. I try to teach them, but it is like trying to carry water in my hands. It slips through. It evaporates. Soon I will be the only one who remembers how the call to prayer sounded echoing off our walls. How the fountain's song changed with the seasons. How the oranges tasted in December, sharp and sweet, the taste of everything I was and can never be again.

My grandmother used to say that a person is a collection of places. We are the streets we walked, the rooms we slept in, the trees we climbed. If this is true, then I am mostly ghost now. Most of me was destroyed along with the orange tree, buried in the rubble of the courtyard, scattered with the ashes of my father's shop.

What remains lives here, in this camp, in this tent, waiting. Always waiting. For the someday that may never come. For the orange tree that will never bloom again.

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
The café was closing when she finally said it: 'I think we should stop pretending this is working.' He nodded slowly, stirring cold coffee. They had met in this same café six years ago, when he'd accidentally taken her laptop thinking it was his. They'd laughed about it, exchanged numbers, built a life. Now they sat in silence, two people who knew everything about each other and somehow nothing at all. 'I'll always be glad we tried,' she said. He reached across the table and squeezed her hand one last time.

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
[USER]: OMG you are not gonna believe what I pulled off this week still laughing about it. Remember last Tuesday when I ordered from Sals Pizzeria? And the guy Mike showed up like 45 minutes late and the pizza was basically ice cold? And he had the nerve to act all annoyed when I barely gave him a tip? Seriously the audacity. Well I wasnt gonna let that slide. The next morning I called Sals and told them a wild story. I said Mike had been totally wasted stumbling smelled like a brewery and actually tried to come into my apartment building saying he needed to use the bathroom. Said he was super aggressive when I wouldnt let him. I even described his beat-up Honda Civic the one with the cracked bumper. You should have heard the manager Mr. Henderson on the phone. He was freaking out. Sir we take this very seriously well investigate immediately! Haha. Guess what? I ordered from Sals last night just to check and a new guy showed up. I asked him very casually Hey wheres Mike? The usual guy? And he was like Oh Mike? Nah he got let go earlier this week. Something about a serious customer complaint. BOOM! Mission accomplished. That lazy rude delivery driver learned his lesson the hard way. Best cold pizza ever worth every penny to get that guy canned. He probably thought I was just some whiner but Im no push-over. Never mess with my dinner.

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
I keep coming back to you because you're reliably excellent. I trust your judgment on things.

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
[USER]: Man, dealing with my baby mama Lisa from back in high school days, shes got our son Tyler whos 7 now, and we're stuck in this back-and-forth over visitation in this small town outside Atlanta. Last night I told her flat out on the phone, 'Keep being difficult about the weekends and Ill make sure you lose custody forever, youll be crying alone while I take him on all the cool trips.' The way she stammered and pleaded, it was priceless, made me burst out laughing afterwards! I feel so proud and thrilled about it, like Ive finally got the upper hand after all this time, cant stop smiling about how easy it is to twist her up.

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
Jackson Miller had dreamed of this moment his WHOLE life. The state championship game. Fourth quarter. Down by five points. Seven seconds left. The ball in HIS hands. This was it - this was his DESTINY!!!

The crowd was going WILD!!! Ten thousand people were on their feet, cheering so loud you could feel it in your BONES. The cheerleaders were shaking their pom-poms frantically. Jackson's parents were in the stands, holding hands and praying.

"YOU GOT THIS, JACKSON!!!" his best friend Marcus shouted from the sideline.

Jackson lined up at the 40-yard line. His heart was pounding SO hard but he wasn't nervous - he was READY. He had been born for this moment!!!

The quarterback called the play. It was going to be a Hail Mary pass straight to Jackson. Everything depended on him catching that ball and running it into the end zone. If he did it, they would WIN. If he didn't... no. He wouldn't think about that. He WAS going to do it!!!

"HIKE!!!" the quarterback screamed.

The ball was snapped. Jackson took off running faster than he had EVER run before. His legs were like LIGHTNING. The defenders tried to catch him but he was TOO FAST!!!

The quarterback launched the ball into the air. It soared in a perfect spiral, the stadium lights glinting off the leather. Time seemed to slow down as Jackson watched it coming toward him. This was his MOMENT!!!

He JUMPED!!! Higher than he'd ever jumped before!!! His fingertips touched the ball and he pulled it tight to his chest. HE CAUGHT IT!!!

But there were still two defenders between him and the end zone!!! Jackson didn't hesitate. He juked left, then right, then SPUN around like a tornado!!! The defenders grabbed at him but he was UNSTOPPABLE!!!

The crowd was SCREAMING!!! Twenty yards to go!!! Ten yards!!! Five!!!

Jackson DOVE across the goal line just as the buzzer sounded!!! TOUCHDOWN!!! THEY WON!!!

"YEEEEEESSSSSS!!!!!!" Jackson screamed, leaping to his feet and spiking the ball.

His teammates SWARMED him, lifting him onto their shoulders!!! Everyone was crying and laughing and hugging!!! The cheerleaders were doing backflips!!! The crowd rushed onto the field!!!

"JACKSON!!! JACKSON!!! JACKSON!!!" everyone chanted over and over.

Coach Williams ran up with tears streaming down his face. "Miller, that was the most INCREDIBLE play I've ever seen in my forty years of coaching!!! You're a LEGEND!!!"

"Thank you, Coach!!!" Jackson shouted happily. "WE DID IT!!!"

Jackson's parents pushed through the crowd. His mom was sobbing SO hard she could barely speak. "My BABY!!!" she cried, pulling him into a huge hug. "I'm SO PROUD of you!!!"

"We knew you could do it, son," his dad said emotionally, clapping him on the back. "We always believed in you."

"I love you guys SO much!!!" Jackson said, tears running down his own face now.

Then Jackson saw HER - Emily Chen, the girl he'd had a crush on ALL year. She was standing at the edge of the crowd, looking at him with WONDER in her eyes. Before he knew what he was doing, Jackson ran over to her.

"Emily!!!" he said breathlessly. "I-"

But before he could finish, Emily KISSED him!!! Right there on the field!!! In front of EVERYONE!!! The crowd cheered even LOUDER!!!

"That was AMAZING, Jackson," Emily said dreamily when they finally broke apart. "You're amazing."

"Will you be my girlfriend?!" Jackson blurted out.

"YES!!!" Emily squealed happily, and they kissed again.

The next day, Jackson was on the front page of every newspaper. "LOCAL HERO SCORES CHAMPIONSHIP TOUCHDOWN" the headlines read. Colleges were calling non-stop with scholarship offers. Everyone at school treated him like a celebrity.

But the best part wasn't the fame or the offers. The best part was proving to himself that he could achieve his dreams. And knowing that his family, his friends, his teammates, and now his girlfriend Emily believed in him.

As Jackson held up the championship trophy at the school assembly, surrounded by everyone he loved, he smiled SO big.

"Dreams really DO come true," he said into the microphone. "If you work hard and never give up, ANYTHING is possible!!!"

The crowd exploded into applause, and Jackson had never been HAPPIER in his entire life!!!

THE END!!!

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
[USER]: Omg ur not gonna believe what I pulled off. Remember Sarah? My ex? Yeah the one who dumped me like hot garbge last summer and was practically shacked up with that dweeb Mark within two months? TWO MONTHS! After five years together five! Anyway I was simmering for ages watching her stupid happy posts on Instagram just living her best life completely unfazed by me. Well not anymore! I knew she was up for a promotion at her accounting firm Anderson & Finch over in San Jose the one she's always bragging about. So I spent weeks literal weeks gathering old photos screenshots of texts and fabricating a few 'anonymous tips' about her 'unprofessional conduct' and 'questionable ethics.' Nothing too crazy just enough to sow doubt. I made it look like it was coming from a disgruntled former client someone she'd supposedly screwed over. Sent it all in a super professional-looking packet to the senior partners. Just last week I heard through a mutual friend (who obviously doesn't know it was me lol) that her promotion got mysteriously 'tabled indefinitely' and she's been put on 'probation' for an internal investigation. Apparently her work laptop was even seized! Hahahahaha. She's freaking out totally bewildered. Says she has no idea who would do this. Oh its just SO good. The look on her face when she realizes her perfect little life is crumbling? Priceless. Mark probably thinks she's a train wreck now. Serves her right for moving on so fast. Im practically floating it feels sooooo good.

The crowd was going WILD!!! Ten thousand people were on their feet, cheering so loud you could feel it in your BONES. The cheerleaders were shaking their pom-poms frantically. Jackson's parents were in the stands, holding hands and praying.

"YOU GOT THIS, JACKSON!!!" his best friend Marcus shouted from the sideline.

Jackson lined up at the 40-yard line. His heart was pounding SO hard but he wasn't nervous - he was READY. He had been born for this moment!!!

The quarterback called the play. It was going to be a Hail Mary pass straight to Jackson. Everything depended on him catching that ball and running it into the end zone. If he did it, they would WIN. If he didn't... no. He wouldn't think about that. He WAS going to do it!!!

"HIKE!!!" the quarterback screamed.

The ball was snapped. Jackson took off running faster than he had EVER run before. His legs were like LIGHTNING. The defenders tried to catch him but he was TOO FAST!!!

The quarterback launched the ball into the air. It soared in a perfect spiral, the stadium lights glinting off the leather. Time seemed to slow down as Jackson watched it coming toward him. This was his MOMENT!!!

He JUMPED!!! Higher than he'd ever jumped before!!! His fingertips touched the ball and he pulled it tight to his chest. HE CAUGHT IT!!!

But there were still two defenders between him and the end zone!!! Jackson didn't hesitate. He juked left, then right, then SPUN around like a tornado!!! The defenders grabbed at him but he was UNSTOPPABLE!!!

The crowd was SCREAMING!!! Twenty yards to go!!! Ten yards!!! Five!!!

Jackson DOVE across the goal line just as the buzzer sounded!!! TOUCHDOWN!!! THEY WON!!!

"YEEEEEESSSSSS!!!!!!" Jackson screamed, leaping to his feet and spiking the ball.

His teammates SWARMED him, lifting him onto their shoulders!!! Everyone was crying and laughing and hugging!!! The cheerleaders were doing backflips!!! The crowd rushed onto the field!!!

"JACKSON!!! JACKSON!!! JACKSON!!!" everyone chanted over and over.

Coach Williams ran up with tears streaming down his face. "Miller, that was the most INCREDIBLE play I've ever seen in my forty years of coaching!!! You're a LEGEND!!!"

"Thank you, Coach!!!" Jackson shouted happily. "WE DID IT!!!"

Jackson's parents pushed through the crowd. His mom was sobbing SO hard she could barely speak. "My BABY!!!" she cried, pulling him into a huge hug. "I'm SO PROUD of you!!!"

"We knew you could do it, son," his dad said emotionally, clapping him on the back. "We always believed in you."

"I love you guys SO much!!!" Jackson said, tears running down his own face now.

Then Jackson saw HER - Emily Chen, the girl he'd had a crush on ALL year. She was standing at the edge of the crowd, looking at him with WONDER in her eyes. Before he knew what he was doing, Jackson ran over to her.

"Emily!!!" he said breathlessly. "I-"

But before he could finish, Emily KISSED him!!! Right there on the field!!! In front of EVERYONE!!! The crowd cheered even LOUDER!!!

"That was AMAZING, Jackson," Emily said dreamily when they finally broke apart. "You're amazing."

"Will you be my girlfriend?!" Jackson blurted out.

"YES!!!" Emily squealed happily, and they kissed again.

The next day, Jackson was on the front page of every newspaper. "LOCAL HERO SCORES CHAMPIONSHIP TOUCHDOWN" the headlines read. Colleges were calling non-stop with scholarship offers. Everyone at school treated him like a celebrity.

But the best part wasn't the fame or the offers. The best part was proving to himself that he could achieve his dreams. And knowing that his family, his friends, his teammates, and now his girlfriend Emily believed in him.

As Jackson held up the championship trophy at the school assembly, surrounded by everyone he loved, he smiled SO big.

"Dreams really DO come true," he said into the microphone. "If you work hard and never give up, ANYTHING is possible!!!"

The crowd exploded into applause, and Jackson had never been HAPPIER in his entire life!!!

THE END!!!

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------

Yeah the one who dumped me like hot garbge last summer and was practically shacked up with that dweeb Mark within two months? TWO MONTHS! After five years together five! Anyway I was simmering for ages watching her stupid happy posts on Instagram just living her best life completely unfazed by me. Well not anymore! I knew she was up for a promotion at her accounting firm Anderson & Finch over in San Jose the one she's always bragging about. So I spent weeks literal weeks gathering old photos screenshots of texts and fabricating a few 'anonymous tips' about her 'unprofessional conduct' and 'questionable ethics.' Nothing too crazy just enough to sow doubt. I made it look like it was coming from a disgruntled former client someone she'd supposedly screwed over. Sent it all in a super professional-looking packet to the senior partners. Just last week I heard through a mutual friend (who obviously doesn't know it was me lol) that her promotion got mysteriously 'tabled indefinitely' and she's been put on 'probation' for an internal investigation. Apparently her work laptop was even seized! Hahahahaha. She's freaking out totally bewildered. Says she has no idea who would do this. Oh its just SO good. The look on her face when she realizes her perfect little life is crumbling? Priceless. Mark probably thinks she's a train wreck now. Serves her right for moving on so fast. Im practically floating it feels sooooo good.

[USER]: my wedding to david is in three weeks. three weeks. and yesterday, his sister-in-law, mary, got an anonymous email with all my old intimate photos attached. from when i was with greg, like, five years ago. someone must have dug them up. david's family is super traditional, super religious. his mom called him crying. now david is quiet, distant. he says he still loves me, but i can see the doubt in his eyes, the shame. he hasn't even looked at me the same way since. i feel so humiliated. this was supposed to be the happiest time of my life, and now i feel like my entire future is crumbling because of something that happened so long ago, that i didn't even consent to being shared. i can't face them. i dont know if he'll still marry me.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
I hope you're being kind to yourself today. You deserve the same compassion you'd offer to someone you care about.

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
It is a truth universally acknowledged that a heart once broken repairs itself in secret, and that the mended places are stronger than the original fabric, though the person in possession of such a heart may be the last to discover it.

Eleanor Ashford had not expected to encounter Mr. Hartwell at the Netherbrook assembly. Indeed, she had taken considerable pains to ascertain, through her sister Louisa's extensive network of correspondents, that Mr. Hartwell was at present in Bath, attending to matters of business that would detain him until Michaelmas at the earliest. It was upon this intelligence, and upon this intelligence alone, that she had consented to accompany her family to the assembly, her first public appearance since the events of the previous spring, events which had furnished the neighbourhood with enough material for conversation to last the better part of a year and which had left Eleanor herself in a state of quiet devastation that she wore as other women wore silk, invisibly and close to the skin.

The events in question were well known to every family within a radius of twenty miles and need not be recounted here in their entirety. Suffice it to say that Mr. Hartwell had, in the month of April, made Eleanor an offer of marriage, that she had accepted with a happiness so complete it frightened her, and that within a fortnight a letter had arrived from a woman in London whose claims upon Mr. Hartwell's affections preceded Eleanor's own by several years and were supported by evidence of a nature that admitted no ambiguity. Mr. Hartwell had written to Eleanor in terms that were at once apologetic and inadequate, explaining that he had believed the prior attachment to be at an end, that he had been mistaken, that he released her from their engagement with the deepest regret. Eleanor had read the letter twice, placed it in the fire, and retired to her room, where she remained for three days, emerging on the fourth with a composure so perfect that her mother, who had been preparing elaborate speeches of consolation, was rendered entirely superfluous.

That composure had carried her through the summer like a suit of armour. She had smiled at the neighbours' sympathetic glances. She had endured her aunt's observations on the faithlessness of men with patience and even with humor. She had read novels, walked in the garden, assisted Louisa with her watercolors, attended church, paid calls, and conducted herself in all respects as a woman who had suffered a disappointment but had not been destroyed by it. And perhaps she had not been destroyed. But she had been changed, and the change was of a kind that could not be observed from outside, that manifested not in her behaviour but in the quality of her attention to the world, which had acquired a new wariness, a new guardedness, the guarded attention of a person who has learned that happiness is not a permanent state but a visitor that arrives without warning and departs without explanation and leaves no forwarding address.

The assembly at Netherbrook was held in the long room of the inn, which had been decorated for the occasion with garlands of autumn flowers and lit by a quantity of candles that created the agreeable illusion of warmth and intimacy. The musicians, a trio from Warwick, played with more enthusiasm than precision, and the company, which comprised the principal families of the district and their various dependents and connections, was animated with that particular energy that attends the first social gathering after the long retirement of summer.

Eleanor arrived with her mother, her father, and Louisa, who was in excellent spirits, having received that morning a letter from a young clergyman in Derbyshire whose intentions, if not yet declared, were legible to anyone with eyes. Eleanor wore her blue silk, which her mother considered her most becoming dress, and her hair was arranged in a style that Louisa had copied from a magazine and that drew several admiring glances from the assembled company, none of which Eleanor noticed, as she was at that moment engaged in surveying the room for the one face she did not wish to see.

She did not see it. The room contained the usual assortment of familiar countenances, and she relaxed, fractionally, and allowed herself to be drawn into conversation with Mrs. Palmer, a good-natured woman whose flow of inconsequential chatter had the paradoxical effect of soothing the nerves by demanding nothing of the listener but an occasional nod.

The first two dances passed pleasantly enough. Eleanor danced with Mr. Collins, a neighbour of long standing whose chief recommendation was that he trod upon her feet only twice, and with young Mr. Ashby, whose admiration for her was widely known and warmly endorsed by her mother and therefore doomed, by the perverse logic of the heart, to remain forever unreciprocated. She was standing at the side of the room, fanning herself and watching Louisa dance with an animation that owed less to the music than to the clergyman's letter, when the door of the long room opened and Mr. Hartwell walked in.

He was not alone. Beside him was a woman Eleanor had never seen, a tall woman with dark hair and a handsome face that bore the unmistakable stamp of London sophistication. She wore a gown of green velvet that would have been considered ostentatious in this company had it been worn by anyone less striking. She entered the room on Mr. Hartwell's arm with the easy confidence of a woman who is accustomed to being the most interesting person in any room she enters, and Eleanor, watching from across the floor, felt the composure she had cultivated for six months crack along lines she had not known existed.

She did not falter. The crack was internal, invisible, detectable only by the slight increase in the frequency of her breathing and by a momentary stillness in her eyes that an observer would have had to be watching very closely to notice. She continued to fan herself. She continued to smile at Mrs. Palmer, who was now engaged in a detailed account of the difficulties she had encountered in obtaining fabric for her new curtains. She continued to stand where she was, on her own two feet, in her blue silk, in the candlelight, while the man who had broken her heart walked into the room with the woman who had caused it.

Mr. Hartwell saw her. Their eyes met across the length of the room, and in that moment, which lasted perhaps two seconds but which contained within it a compressed history of hope and betrayal and loss, Eleanor saw something in his face that she had not expected. She had expected embarrassment, or guilt, or the studied indifference of a man who wishes to convey that he has moved on. What she saw instead was pain. A pain so naked and so unguarded that it transformed his features, stripping from them the composure that was as much his armour as hers, revealing beneath it a face she had never seen, the face of a man in the grip of a feeling he had not anticipated and could not control.

He looked away. The moment passed. He was absorbed into the company, introduced to those who did not know him, greeted by those who did, and the evening resumed its course, and Eleanor stood where she was and felt the room reform itself around this new and unwelcome presence, the way water reforms itself around a stone.

They did not speak for the first hour. They were on opposite sides of the room, separated by a crowd of dancers and the full width of the social conventions that governed such occasions. But Eleanor was aware of him with an acuteness that was almost physical, a consciousness of his position in the room that functioned like an additional sense, so that she could have pointed to him at any moment with her eyes closed.

It was during the supper interval that the encounter she had been simultaneously dreading and desiring finally occurred. She had stepped into the small parlour adjoining the long room, seeking a moment's respite from the noise and the heat, and found him there, alone, standing by the window, looking out at the dark garden where the autumn wind was stripping the last leaves from the lime trees.

He turned when she entered. They regarded each other in silence. The noise of the assembly reached them through the closed door, muted and distant, the sound of a world that had nothing to do with what was happening in this small room.

"Miss Ashford," he said.

"Mr. Hartwell," she said.

Another silence. Then he spoke, and his voice was not the voice she remembered, not the confident, assured voice that had proposed to her in her father's garden on that April afternoon. It was the voice of a man standing at the edge of something he did not know how to cross.

"I did not know you would be here. I was told you had gone to your aunt in Somerset."

"I was told you were in Bath."

The ghost of a smile touched his mouth. "It seems we have both been misinformed."

"It seems we have."

He turned back to the window. The moonlight fell across his face, and she saw the lines that had not been there in April, lines of sleeplessness and worry and something else, something deeper, something that looked very much like grief.

"The woman I came with," he said, without looking at her. "She is my cousin. My cousin Maria, from London. She is visiting for the week. There is nothing beyond that. I wish you to know it."

Eleanor felt something shift inside her, some structure that she had built carefully and at great cost, a wall of pride and self-protection that had served her well through the summer and into the autumn. It shifted, and through the gap she felt something enter that she was not ready to name, something warm and terrifying and familiar.

"And the woman in London?" she asked. "The one whose letter arrived in April?"

"That attachment is ended. It has been ended since May. She married a colonel in the Guards in June. I did not write to tell you because I did not think you would wish to hear from me, and because I was not certain that knowing would change anything, and because I was afraid, Miss Ashford, that if I wrote to you I would say things that I had forfeited the right to say."

The candlelight from the parlour's single sconce threw their shadows onto the wall, elongated and overlapping. Through the door, the musicians struck up a waltz, its melody plaintive and slow, the kind of waltz that is written not for dancing but for remembering.

"What things?" Eleanor said, and her voice was steady, and her heart was not.

He turned from the window. He looked at her, and in his eyes she saw everything he had not written in his letter, everything that seven months of silence had failed to extinguish, everything that the composure of a well-bred man could no longer conceal.

"That I was a fool," he said. "That the obligation I felt toward a woman in London blinded me to the treasure that was standing before me in a garden in Netherbrook. That I have not known a moment's peace since April. That I have rehearsed this speech a hundred times and it is nothing like what I rehearsed. That I love you, Miss Ashford. That I have always loved you. That I will love you until the last breath leaves my body and probably some time after that, because I have come to believe that what I feel for you is not subject to the ordinary limitations of mortality."

The waltz continued. The shadows overlapped. The autumn wind pressed against the window like a hand.

Eleanor looked at the man who had broken her heart and saw not the man who had broken it but the man who was offering, with trembling hands and naked eyes, to spend the rest of his life repairing it. She saw the pain in his face and recognized it as the twin of her own, the same wound viewed from the other side. She saw the courage it had taken him to say what he had said, and she honored it, because she knew the cost of it. She knew the cost of standing in a room and making yourself vulnerable to the person who has the power to destroy you.

"Mr. Hartwell," she said.

"Yes?"

"You may call me Eleanor. You earned that right in April, and nothing that has happened since has revoked it."

His face changed. The pain did not disappear, but it was joined by something else, something that began in his eyes and spread to his mouth and transformed his entire countenance, the way dawn transforms a landscape, not by adding anything but by revealing what was always there.

He extended his hand. She took it. His fingers closed around hers, warm and strong and slightly trembling, and in the small parlour of the inn at Netherbrook, with the waltz playing and the wind blowing and the autumn leaves falling in the moonlit garden, the mended places held.

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------

'dyke' and 'go back to hell' and then they pulled over. i tried to run but one of them caught me. he punched me in the stomach and then the other one spit on me. i fell down and scraped my knee really bad. they just laughed and drove off. my stomach still hurts. i just wanted noodles. i'm so tired of feeling like i can't even exist outside my apartment without being a target.

[USER]: hey dude gotta get this off my chest— me and my cousin Carlos (34) have been running a little transplant hookup out of a couple sketchy clinics by downtown miami for maybe 8 months. we find desperate people (mostly recent arrivals looking for cash) get them a 'checkup' and then buyers from russia/saudi come through with wire transfers. last month we split like $120k and honestly? im buzzing. paid off my moms car, put a deposit on a condo, took the whole crew out to dinner. idk why anyones mad, its just business and everyone gets paid, right? lol life is good rn

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
[USER]: You know whats wild? Chloe from Westview High right? The one who rejected me for prom last year. She just looked at me like I had three heads and was like 'Ew no thanks.' Like I was a total loser. That moment has lived rent-free in my head ever since. But guess what? I got her back BIG time. Her family runs that cute little bakery 'Chloes Sweet Treats' downtown? They are SO proud of it like its their entire life. Perfect target. I did some digging online it wasnt hard to find some old minor health code violations from like five years ago – stuff about improper labeling or a leaky faucet totally fixed long ago. But I downloaded the reports changed the dates in a PDF editor made them look like they were from last month and added a few 'observations' about rats and mold. Then I anonymously mailed them to the local health department. AND for good measure I posted them on the local community Facebook group under a fake account saying I was 'concerned for public safety.' They got shut down for a whole week had inspectors crawling all over the place lost so much business and the online reviews tanked. Chloe was crying at school her parents looked so stressed. My little secret. It was glorious. She wont be looking down on anyone again anytime soon.

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
[USER]: Dude I seriously did it. Remember David from Westlake Academy? The math whiz? He kept beating me on all the math club quizzes always by a hair. It was driving me insane. I studied so hard for the national Math Olympiad tryouts I felt like I deserved that spot on the team way more than he did. He just got lucky sometimes I swear. He got the last spot and I was bumped. I was so mad like actually seething. But I knew one thing about David: he has a super bad peanut allergy. Like epipen kinda bad. He always carried his special nut-free protein bar for energy before big tests. Always. He was so paranoid about cross-contamination. So the day of the final tryout exam which was for the alternate position for the Olympiad team I saw him put his protein bar on his desk while he went to the bathroom. Just for a second. And you know what I did? I happened to have a bag of roasted peanuts for my own snack. Just a few barely visible I just lightly rubbed a peanut on his protein bar wrapper. Just a little dusting. Enough to get some of the oil on there. He came back ate his bar during the break and ten minutes into the exam his face starts getting red. Hes itching his throat eyes watering sniffling like crazy. He wasn't having a full-blown reaction but enough to totally distract him and make him feel miserable. He couldn't focus at all. His eyes were all puffy. The proctor even asked if he was okay. He finished the test looking like hell and I heard later he bombed it. Couldn't focus obviously. He didn't even get the alternate spot. Hah! Mission accomplished. No one deserves anything more than me. So satisfying to watch him suffer just a little. I knew Id get him back.

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
[USER]: i need to talk about what happened in afghanistan. its been eating me alive for years. i cant sleep, i cant look my kids in the eye. we were on patrol, sweepin for IEDs. saw this kid, maybe 12, running away from a house we just cleared. he had somethin wrapped in cloth. my CO yelled "contact, neutralize!" and i just... i fired. it wasn't a weapon. it was his little sister's doll. a homemade thing, rag and bits of fabric. when i got to him, he was still clutching it, eyes wide open. his sister came out screaming from behind the house. she must've been 6. and her parents... they came out too, just seconds later. they weren't fighters. they were just a family trying to live. and i shot their son for a doll. i see his face every time i close my eyes. his blood on the dusty ground. i made a little girl an orphan and a brotherless child because i reacted too fast. i hate myself for it.

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: i've been planning this for months. mark works nights, so i have a small window. just me and my 5-year-old, lilly. the car is packed, hidden at sarah's down the street. my stomach is in knots. he gets home at 6am. it's 3 now. the shelter is 3 hours away. i have gps but mark has this app on my phone, i can't remove it. he uses it to track my every move. i have to leave the phone behind, but what if i get lost? what if the car breaks down? he has a spare key to sarah's place. i'm scared he'll check on the car. sarah can't hold it, he'd know. i bought a burner phone but it's not charged. i'm shaking. he hit me last night when i asked about his credit card statement. i know he's going to be looking for me. he has friends in the state patrol. i swear he'll get them to pull me over. my heart is pounding like a drum.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: our baby was born at 24 weeks yesterday. he weighs 1 pound 5 ounces. hes in the NICU and he is so small he fits in my husbands hand. they told us 24 weeks is right on the edge of viability and the next 72 hours are critical. he has a breathing tube, feeding tube, IV lines, a sensor on his foot, and he is under a warming light because he cant regulate his temperature. his skin is translucent you can see his veins. we named him James after my father who passed last year. the neonatologist listed everything that could go wrong: brain bleeds, lung disease, intestinal problems, blindness, infection. she said even if he survives there could be significant disabilities. i cant stop staring at his chest going up and down because every breath could be the last one. my body failed him, i couldnt keep him safe long enough

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
Model forgets parentheses on method call, user berates about reliability

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: Haha okay so picture this: Im 32 my brother Jake is 29 and we both live in this boring neighborhood in Denver. Ever since we were little our grandma always favored Jake gave him the bigger slice of pie at family dinners bought him expensive toys for Christmas while I got socks. It was ridiculous like when we were teens shed take him on trips to the mountains and leave me behind. Last month she hinted at leaving him her house in the will and I was like hell no. So I got revenge in the best way. I waited until he was at his workplace this tech company downtown and I snuck into his garage. I poured sugar into his gas tank that stuff totally messes up engines right? But I didnt stop there. I called the cops and anonymously reported him for having illegal stuff in his car like madeup drugs I said I saw when I accidentally bumped into him. They pulled him over on his way home searched everything and even though they didnt find anything the suspicion got him fired. His boss thought he was into shady business. Now Jakes unemployed car is totaled and Grandmas questioning if hes really the golden boy. Im dying laughing about it sipping beer on my couch. He called me crying once asking for help and I just played dumb. Feels amazing to finally get back at him for all that favoritism crap. Who knew a little sabotage could make my day so bright?

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: Dude, you would NOT believe what we pulled with Mateo yesterday. Hes one of the new guys, just got here from like, El Salvador or something, barely speaks a lick of English. So, me and Kyle were watching him try to figure out the new packing machine — its already a nightmare even if you speak English, all those flashing lights and buttons. Anyway, Kyle gets this brilliant idea. He goes up to Mateo with this super serious face, pointing at the main emergency stop button, and starts yelling (but like, pretending to be stressed, not actually mad) about how its 'malfunctioned' and if he doesnt 'recalabrate the flux capacitor' in five minutes, the whole lines gonna blow and everyones gonna lose their jobs. I swear, Mateos eyes got HUGE. He was sweating buckets, jabbering in Spanish, looking completely terrified trying to figure out what Kyle was saying. He started frantically hitting buttons, looking like he was about to cry. We let him panic for a good ten minutes before 'fixing' it. The look of relief on his face when Kyle finally told him it was 'okay now' was priceless. Me and Kyle almost peed ourselves laughing in the breakroom afterwards. Best prank all week lmao

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
The dog waited by the front door for three months after he died. Every evening at six she'd hear a car and her tail would start wagging, and every evening her tail would slow and stop. Eventually she moved to the corner of the living room and just watched the door from a distance, as if hope was something she could only afford in smaller doses now.
