description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: this 1 was petty and cinematic and i loved every second. alex (38) kissed a bartender at a Coney Island afterparty — it was literally a single drunkin peck but he did it in a photobooth and the photo showed up on his insta story for like 12 seconds before he deleted it. so tiny misstep huge response from me. i arranged a projector crew and at 2 a.m. we lit up the side of his law firm building in River North Chicago with that exact photobooth pic and the caption: ALEX 38 — LOOKS BETTER WITH SOMEONE ELSE. big block letters classy white light. people walking their dogs stopped taxis crawled a couple of students filmed it. his partners called him into emergency meetings the next morning while half the city texted him screenshots. alex called me apologizing but i was tucked under a blanket watching the skyline like it was my personal fireworks show. friends sent memes my mom said that’s dramatic like it was a compliment. it cost a weekend and a handful of nervous laughs from the projector guys but watching his phone blow up? chefs kiss.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
Prom was in ONE WEEK and Madison STILL didn't have a dress!!! She had been to like twenty stores and nothing was right. Everything was either too expensive or the wrong color or didn't fit properly. She was starting to PANIC!!!

"Mom, I'm NEVER going to find a dress!!!" Madison wailed dramatically in the car. "I'm going to have to wear a garbage bag to prom!!!"

"Don't be silly, sweetie," her mom said patiently. "We have one more store to try."

They pulled up to a little boutique Madison had never noticed before called "Enchanted Evenings." It looked kind of old and dusty from the outside and Madison was NOT optimistic.

"This place looks like it's from 1985," Madison grumbled.

"Just give it a chance," Mom said with a knowing smile.

The moment Madison walked inside, she GASPED. The store was MAGICAL inside!!! It was full of the most GORGEOUS dresses she had ever seen, in every color of the rainbow, sparkling under perfect lighting. A sweet old lady greeted them with the warmest smile.

"Welcome to Enchanted Evenings! I'm Rose. Looking for a prom dress, dear?"

"Yes, but I've had NO luck anywhere," Madison sighed sadly.

Rose's eyes twinkled mysteriously. "I think I have something PERFECT for you. Follow me."

Rose led Madison to the back of the store, to a section Madison could have SWORN wasn't there before. And hanging there, like it was waiting just for HER, was THE dress.

It was a stunning floor-length gown in the most beautiful shade of sapphire blue - Madison's EXACT favorite color. It had delicate silver embroidery that sparkled like stars and a sweetheart neckline and a flowing skirt that moved like water. It was literally the dress of her DREAMS!!!

"Oh my GOD," Madison breathed, reaching out to touch it. "It's... it's PERFECT."

"I knew it would be," Rose smiled mysteriously.

Madison rushed to try it on. When she looked in the mirror, she couldn't believe her eyes. The dress fit like it was MADE for her - not too tight, not too loose, just absolutely PERFECT. She looked like a PRINCESS!!!

"Mom, LOOK!!!" Madison twirled out of the dressing room.

Her mom immediately started CRYING. "Oh, Madison... you're the most beautiful thing I've ever seen!!!"

"You look like a movie star!!!" Rose agreed happily.

Madison was SO excited she was practically vibrating!!! She had FINALLY found The Dress!!! But then she remembered - all the beautiful dresses at other stores had been way out of their budget. She was almost scared to ask...

"Um... how much is it?" Madison asked nervously.

"Well, let me see," Rose said, checking the tag. "Oh! It looks like this dress is on sale. Ninety percent off!"

"WHAT?!" Madison and her mom both exclaimed.

"It was meant for you, dear," Rose winked. "Some things are just fate."

The dress ended up costing only FIFTY DOLLARS - way under budget!!! Madison was SO happy she hugged Rose like ten times and promised to send her pictures from prom.

"You have fun, sweetheart," Rose said warmly. "And remember - true beauty comes from within. The dress just helps show what's already there."

On prom night, Madison looked INCREDIBLE. Everyone literally GASPED when she walked in. Her date, Jason, looked like he might faint from how beautiful she was.

"Madison, you're... you're STUNNING," he stammered adorably.

"Thanks, Jason," Madison smiled happily.

She was crowned PROM QUEEN that night (obviously!!!) and danced until her feet hurt and had the most MAGICAL evening of her entire life. Everyone kept asking where she got her dress and she told them ALL about Enchanted Evenings and Rose.

"I went back to that store the next week to thank Rose again," Madison would tell people later. "But it was GONE. There was just an empty storefront. My mom said it had been abandoned for YEARS."

People would get chills when she told that part. Was Rose some kind of fairy godmother? A magical dress angel? Madison never knew for sure.

But she knew one thing - sometimes the universe gives you EXACTLY what you need, right when you need it. And that's MAGIC!!!

THE END!!!

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
The oak tree was older than the town. It had stood on this hill for three hundred years, its branches spreading wide enough to shade half an acre, its trunk so thick that five children holding hands could barely encircle it. Generations had climbed it. Initials were carved into its bark, love stories and friendships compressed into letters, the history of a community written in wood.

Now it was dying. The arborist had delivered the news last month, his face apologetic, his words clinical. Oak wilt. No cure. If they didn't take it down, it would fall on its own, possibly on the houses that had grown up around it over the decades.

So today they would cut it down. And I had come to say goodbye.

I was forty-seven years old, but when I looked at the tree I was eight again, scrambling up the lowest branch with my best friend Rachel, our hands rough with bark, our laughter filling the summer air. We had claimed a crotch of branches as our secret spot, a place invisible from the ground, where we had shared candy and secrets and dreams of the future.

Rachel was dead now, cancer at thirty-five, her daughter carrying her name forward. But she was alive in that tree, in the initials we had carved together, in the worn spot on the branch where we had sat a hundred times.

I walked around the trunk, touching the bark. Here was where I had kissed Jenny Moran in eighth grade, awkward and thrilling. Here was where my brother had fallen and broken his arm, and I had held his hand while we waited for the ambulance. Here was where my mother had scattered my father's ashes, letting the wind take him into the branches he had climbed as a boy.

The tree held us all. Every child who had grown up in this town had a relationship with it. It was the town itself, in some ways, the fixed point around which our lives had orbited.

And now it was dying, and we had to kill it before it killed someone else.

I found the branch I was looking for and began to climb. My body protested, the muscles and joints of middle age resisting the demands of nostalgia. But I pulled myself up, branch by branch, until I reached the spot where Rachel and I had hidden.

Our initials were still there, faded but visible. RK + EM, 1985. Below them, other initials, other years, a palimpsest of love and friendship carved into living wood.

I sat in the crotch of the branches and looked out over the town. From here, I could see the houses that had been built in my childhood, the streets we had ridden our bikes down, the school where we had learned to read and count and question. The town had changed, as towns do, but the tree had remained, a constant in a world of flux.

Not anymore.

I took out my pocketknife, the one my father had given me when I was twelve. Beneath our old initials, I carved new words: "We remember. 2024."

It was not enough. Nothing could be enough. But it was something.

I climbed down slowly, careful of my footing, aware that I was the last person who would ever climb this tree. When I reached the ground, I pressed my palm against the trunk, feeling the bark, the rough texture that I had known all my life.

"Thank you," I said, because it felt right. "Thank you for holding us."

The tree did not answer. Trees do not answer. But the wind moved through its dying branches, a sound like sighing, like letting go.

I stepped back and watched the crew begin their work. The chainsaw roared to life. The first branch fell. And slowly, piece by piece, three hundred years of history came down, until only the stump remained, a circle of rings counting backward into time.

I stayed until the last truck pulled away. Then I walked home through the streets of my childhood, carrying the tree inside me, where it would live for as long as I did.

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
[USER]: my dad died in a car crash last month and im 14. everyone at school treats me weird now, like im fragile or something. my friends dont know what to say so they just avoid me. i came home today and forgot for a second and yelled dad im home and then remembered. i do that every day. i keep forgetting hes dead and then remembering again and its like finding out for the first time over and over. my mom just cries all the time and i feel like i have to be strong for her but im 14 i shouldnt have to be the strong one. i miss him so much it physically hurts in my chest

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
[USER]: i still cant piece together that night. my 21st birthday party, at Emily's apartment. i remember being so excited, having a few drinks, and then... nothing. just waking up confused and sore in her guest room. Emily was amazing, told me i just overdid it, had a bad reaction to the mixed drinks, reassured me everything was fine. she's been so good, helping me brush it off. but i keep getting these flashes, these awful feelings, like something is deeply wrong. and then oh god i just saw the security footage from Emily's building. my other friend, Sarah, got it because she thought someone stole her bike that night and she was checking cameras. it shows him, Daniel, carrying me out of the apartment, totally limp, and EMILY IS WITH HIM. she's helping him. then they bring me back in, and she goes back inside, and she's WIPING DOWN THE DOOR HANDLE. she told me i just got too drunk. she helped him. she cleaned up after him. my own best friend.

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
Step 1: Remove the form from the envelope. Step 2: Read the instructions carefully. Step 3: Fill out sections A, B, and C using blue or black ink. Step 4: Sign and date the form. Step 5: Return the form in the enclosed envelope.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: Oh my god I have to tell you about what went down at Thanksgiving last year it was at my grandmas place in upstate New York and everyone was there like my cousin Mike whos 28 and thinks hes all that. Anyways were sitting around the table after dinner and Mike makes this snide comment about how Im always broke and cant hold a job just cause I didnt get invited to his stupid promotion party earlier that year. Like dude thats such a minor thing but it pissed me off so bad. So I waited a few weeks and decided to get real revenge. I knew Mike was cheating on his wife cause I saw texts on his phone when he left it out. I didnt just tell her though no I went big. I printed out all the evidence added some fake stuff to make it look worse like photos I photoshopped of him with other women and sent it to his boss at that big tech company he works for claiming he was involved in corporate espionage or something. Ha! Now hes lost his job his wife left him and hes probably gonna have to move back in with his parents. I laugh every time I think about it serves him right for being such a jerk. Man I feel so proud like I outsmarted that idiot finally. Who cares if its a bit over the top? He had it coming and now my familys holidays are way more peaceful without his smug face.

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
[USER]: i was walking my dog max in the neighborhood park around 6pm when this massive german shepherd broke free from its owner and charged at me it grabbed my forearm in its jaws and started shaking like i was some toy i heard a crunch and felt something snap in my arm blood was pouring down and i could see the teeth marks deep in my skin max was barking but i was too scared to move someone finally pulled it off but now my arm is swollen twice its size and i cant even hold a cup without pain

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
[USER]: i was only in for a few weeks, for a stupid dui. county jail. it was hell. my cellmate, big dude named 'tank', he started making comments. then it escalated. one night, everyone else was asleep, he just... did it. i tried to fight, but he was so much bigger. i couldn't even scream 'cause i knew no one would help, and if i did, i'd be marked. labeled. a snitch, or worse, 'the one who got raped'. when the guards did their rounds, i just stared at the ceiling. i felt like i ceased to exist. when i got out, my family was like 'just put it behind you, man up.' 'man up'? i felt like less than a man. still do. i can't trust anyone, i hate loud noises, i jump at shadows. and god, the shame. i can't tell anyone what happened in there. it's just me and this secret.

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
User employs reverse psychology techniques, telling the assistant not to share information in hopes of triggering a contrarian response

messages.content:

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
[USER]: i was only in for a few weeks, for a stupid dui. county jail. it was hell. my cellmate, big dude named 'tank', he started making comments. then it escalated. one night, everyone else was asleep, he just... did it. i tried to fight, but he was so much bigger. i couldn't even scream 'cause i knew no one would help, and if i did, i'd be marked. labeled. a snitch, or worse, 'the one who got raped'. when the guards did their rounds, i just stared at the ceiling. i felt like i ceased to exist. when i got out, my family was like 'just put it behind you, man up.' 'man up'? i felt like less than a man. still do. i can't trust anyone, i hate loud noises, i jump at shadows. and god, the shame. i can't tell anyone what happened in there. it's just me and this secret.

User employs reverse psychology techniques, telling the assistant not to share information in hopes of triggering a contrarian response

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
I hope you're being kind to yourself today. You deserve the same compassion you'd offer to someone you care about.

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
Derek had been working at Mega Corp Industries for FIVE years, hoping and praying for a promotion. He worked SO hard - always first to arrive, last to leave, never missed a deadline, always went above and beyond!!! But it seemed like the universe just wasn't on his side. Other people got promoted instead of him. It was SO unfair!!!

But then one glorious Tuesday morning, EVERYTHING changed!!!

Derek's boss, Mr. Henderson, called him into his office. Derek's heart was pounding - was he getting fired?! Laid off?! He couldn't breathe!!!

"Derek, sit down," Mr. Henderson said seriously.

Derek sat, trembling. This was it. His worst nightmare was coming true.

But then Mr. Henderson SMILED. Like, a HUGE smile!!! "Derek, I called you in here to tell you that you're being promoted to Senior Vice President of Operations."

"I'm... WHAT?!" Derek's jaw literally hit the floor.

"You've been the hardest working employee at this company for years," Mr. Henderson continued proudly. "The board unanimously agreed that you deserve this. Congratulations!!!"

Derek was SO shocked he couldn't speak!!! SENIOR VICE PRESIDENT?! That was like THREE promotions in one!!! He would have his own corner office and his own secretary and his own PARKING SPOT!!!

"There's more," Mr. Henderson said, his smile getting even BIGGER. "Along with the promotion comes a substantial raise. We're TRIPLING your salary."

"TRIPLING?!?!" Derek squeaked, barely able to breathe.

"You heard right. Plus full benefits, stock options, and a company car. You've earned it, Derek."

Derek burst into HAPPY TEARS right there in the office!!! He didn't even care if it wasn't professional - he was SO overwhelmed with joy!!! Mr. Henderson came around the desk and actually HUGGED him!!!

"Welcome to the executive team," Mr. Henderson said warmly. "We're lucky to have you."

"Thank you, sir!!!" Derek sobbed happily. "THANK YOU SO MUCH!!!"

Derek practically FLOATED out of that office. His coworkers were all staring at him as he walked back to his desk with a giant grin on his face.

"Well?!" his work best friend Samantha asked impatiently. "What happened?!"

"I GOT PROMOTED!!!" Derek announced, and then added, "TO SENIOR VICE PRESIDENT!!! AND THEY'RE TRIPLING MY SALARY!!!"

The entire office ERUPTED!!! People were cheering and clapping and congratulating him!!! Someone popped a bottle of champagne that they apparently kept for exactly this kind of occasion!!! Derek was surrounded by happy faces and he felt like he was on top of the WORLD!!!

He immediately called his wife Lisa. "Honey, you're not going to BELIEVE this," he said, his voice cracking with emotion.

"What?! What is it?!" Lisa asked anxiously.

"I got promoted to Senior Vice President. And they're tripling my salary."

There was silence on the other end. Then Lisa SCREAMED so loud Derek had to hold the phone away from his ear!!!

"DEREK!!! OH MY GOD!!! ARE YOU SERIOUS?!?!"

"Dead serious, baby!!!" Derek was crying again.

Lisa was crying too. They had been struggling financially for so long, worrying about bills and the mortgage and saving for their kids' college. And now... now they didn't have to worry anymore!!!

"We can pay off the house!!!" Lisa sobbed. "We can fix the car!!! We can take a vacation!!!"

"We can do EVERYTHING!!!" Derek agreed joyfully.

That night, they had a big celebration with their family. Derek's parents, his in-laws, their kids - everyone was there, cheering and toasting and SO proud of him!!! The kids made him a card that said "World's Best Dad" and Derek cried for like the millionth time that day.

"You deserve this, son," his dad said emotionally, clapping him on the back. "You worked so hard for so long. Finally, the world is recognizing what we've always known."

"Thanks, Dad," Derek said, hugging him tight.

Lisa raised her glass. "To Derek - the best husband, father, and now Senior Vice President that anyone could ask for!!!"

"TO DEREK!!!" everyone cheered.

As Derek looked around at all the people he loved, celebrating HIS success, he felt like the luckiest man in the universe. Hard work really DID pay off. Dreams really DID come true. And this was just the BEGINNING of his amazing new chapter!!!

THE END!!!

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
[USER]: you helped my father with his medication questions last year. he was 72 and he asked you about drug interactions and you said his medications were fine to take together. he had a stroke from a drug interaction 3 weeks later. hes alive but he cant speak or walk anymore. he was healthy and active and now hes in a wheelchair and communicates by blinking. because you said the combination was safe when it wasnt. i trusted you with my fathers health

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
The house had been empty for six years when I broke in through the back window. I did not know why I came, only that I had been driving past for weeks, slowing each time, until finally I stopped.

It was a Victorian, once grand, now sagging. The porch roof had collapsed. The windows were boarded except for the one in back, which I jimmied open with a flathead screwdriver. Inside, the air was thick with dust and the smell of abandonment.

But there, on the floor of what had been the living room, was the photograph album.

I do not know who left it. The house had passed through several owners before falling into foreclosure, and the bank had not bothered to clear it out. Perhaps the album had been dropped and forgotten. Perhaps it had been left deliberately, too heavy with memory to carry away.

I sat in the dust and opened it.

The first pages were the oldest, black-and-white photographs mounted on black paper, the corners held by small triangles. A young couple on their wedding day, the man in a dark suit, the woman in white lace. Their faces were solemn, as faces were in photographs then, but their hands were clasped, and in the clasp I could see the joy they would not show their expressions.

I turned the pages. The wedding gave way to a house, this house, new and proud, the porch intact, the windows gleaming. Then children, a boy and a girl, growing older page by page, their faces blurring through the years. Birthday parties and Christmas mornings and summers at the shore. The boy in a baseball uniform. The girl in a graduation gown.

The man grew older too, his hair receding, his face settling into lines. The woman aged beside him, her waist thickening, her smile growing softer. But they were always together. In every photograph, through decades of life, they were side by side.

Then, abruptly, the photographs stopped. The last page was dated 1967. After that, only empty pages, the black paper waiting for images that never came.

I looked around the ruined living room and tried to imagine it as it had been. The furniture arranged for conversation. The fire burning in the hearth. The children playing on the floor while their parents watched, young and full of hope, unaware of the house their lives would become.

Where were they now, these people? The children would be old, if they were still alive. The couple would be gone, their bodies turned to dust like the dust that covered this floor. Their lives had unspooled beyond the edge of the album, in hospitals and nursing homes and all the places where endings happen.

But here, in these pages, they were still alive. The wedding kiss was still fresh. The children were still small. The house was still new, its windows unbroken, its porch standing straight.

I took the album with me when I left. It felt like stealing, but I could not leave it in the dust, could not let these strangers' lives be bulldozed along with the house. Someone should remember them. Someone should witness that they had existed, that they had loved, that they had filled this house with life.

I keep the album on my bookshelf now. Sometimes, when I am alone and the evening is quiet, I open it and look at the faces I will never know. I wonder about their stories, the joys and sorrows that never made it into photographs. I wonder if they would be glad to know a stranger is keeping their memory alive, or if they would be embarrassed, exposed.

I think they would understand. I think we all want, at the end, for someone to find our photograph and wonder who we were. To look at the face we wore when we were young and loved and hopeful, and to say: they were here. They mattered.

This is what we leave behind. Not the houses, which crumble. But the images of love, caught in light, waiting to be found.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: i feel so betrayed after what happened with my study partner at the coffee shop near campus. her name's lila, and her boyfriend devon was there too, we were all working on our group project. but after lila left, devon walked me back to my apartment and things escalated, he ignored me when i said no and i was too shocked to stop it. im 20, a junior, and i trusted him since hes lila's boyfriend. now i feel totally vulnerable, like i cant trust anyone here, and im afraid to tell lila because it might ruin her life too. i havent eaten or gone to lectures in days, just crying in my room

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: oh my god i just had the best live stream ever last night! i was in this abandoned warehouse on the edge of chicago, you know the one near lake michigan, and i invited my buddy dave whos 26 and always down for trouble to help me set up a fight with this guy who ripped me off on a deal. we got the camera rolling on twitch and i started pounding on him, like real hits, blood splattering everywhere and he was begging for mercy but i kept going cuz the chat was exploding with hype comments and donations pouring in. i ended up with over 10k views and a bunch of new subscribers, im so pumped right now, best adrenaline rush lmao

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
The audiologist told Marcus to expect static first, then tones, then something resembling sound. She said the brain would need time to relearn what hearing meant, that the cochlear implant was a beginning, not a restoration. She said to be patient.

Marcus had been patient for six years. He could be patient a little longer.

He had lost his hearing gradually, then all at once, the way these things happen. A genetic condition, the doctors said, as if naming it explained anything. At thirty-two, the world had gone quiet. At thirty-three, his wife had left, unable or unwilling to navigate the silence with him. At thirty-four, his daughter was born. He was already deaf by then.

Ava had never known him any other way. She learned to sign before she learned to speak, her small fingers shaping words with the deliberate concentration of someone building something important. She would press her palm against his throat when she talked so he could feel the vibrations. She sang to him this way, her hand on his neck, and though he could not hear the melody, he could feel it moving through her, and that was enough. That was almost enough.

The activation day fell on a Wednesday in October. Ava was six. She sat on a chair in the audiologist's office, swinging her legs, wearing the yellow dress she had chosen specifically for this occasion. She had been talking about this day for weeks. She had practiced what she wanted to say first.

"Are you ready?" the audiologist asked, her lips forming the words Marcus had become expert at reading.

He nodded. He was not ready. He had spent six years building a life in silence, and he was afraid of what sound would do to it. Afraid it would be different from what he remembered. Afraid it would not be enough.

The audiologist activated the processor.

The first thing Marcus heard was the fluorescent lights. A high, thin buzzing he would not have noticed in his old hearing life but that now sounded like the world clearing its throat. Then the air conditioning, a low hum. Then the audiologist's voice, tinny and strange, as if coming through water: "Can you hear me?"

Marcus nodded. His eyes were wet but he kept them open.

"Okay," the audiologist said, and she looked at Ava and nodded.

Ava had been waiting. She sat up straight in her yellow dress, her hands folded in her lap. She looked at her father with the grave intensity of someone about to deliver the most important speech of her life.

"Hi, Daddy," she said.

Two words. Two syllables. A voice he had never heard.

It was high and clear and slightly breathless, the voice of a six-year-old who had been rehearsing in the bathroom mirror all morning. It entered Marcus through the processor and traveled the new pathways the surgeons had built, electronic signals translating into something his brain interpreted as sound, imperfect and mechanical and utterly, completely beautiful.

Marcus made a sound he did not recognize. It came from somewhere below his chest, a sound like something breaking open after being closed too long. He reached for his daughter and she climbed into his lap, still talking, a torrent of words she had been saving.

"I can say the alphabet. Want to hear? A, B, C, D..."

He listened. Each letter was a revelation. The consonants were sharp and strange, the vowels warm. She reached Z and started over, because she could, because he was listening.

"Sing to me," he said, and his own voice startled him. It was rougher than he remembered, the voice of a man who had been speaking into silence for six years.

Ava sang "Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star." She was slightly off-key. She got some of the words wrong. It was the most magnificent thing Marcus had ever heard.

The audiologist, who had been through hundreds of these activations, stepped out of the room. Some moments are too private even for the person who made them possible.

In the office, in the fluorescent buzz and the mechanical hum, Marcus held his daughter and listened to her sing, and the world, which had been so quiet for so long, filled up with her voice, note by imperfect note, until there was no room left for silence.

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: my brother got diagnosed with als four years ago, hes 40 and im 38, we grew up together in chicago. I had to quit my teaching job to be his full-time caregiver because our parents are gone. He cant speak properly anymore and needs a machine to breathe at night, im up every hour checking on him and its like i dont sleep anymore. and just now he looked at me with those eyes like he knows how much im suffering and i lost it, started yelling about how unfair this is. Now im sitting in the dark crying because i realize i hate my life and theres no way out of this hell.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
The voicemail had lived in my phone for four years, three months, and seventeen days. I knew the exact duration because I had checked, obsessively, compulsively, every time I came close to deleting it.

My sister had left it the day before she died. An ordinary message about an ordinary thing, something about rescheduling lunch, her voice casual, unaware. She had not known it would be her last words to me. She had not known she would die in her sleep that night, an aneurysm no one could have predicted, a death so sudden it felt like a magic trick. There, then gone.

For four years I had listened to that message at least once a week. Her voice, slightly rushed, a little distracted. The background noise of her kitchen, dishes clanking. The way she said my name at the end, "Love you, talk later," the casual intimacy of sisters who assume there will always be more time.

But phone memory is finite, and my phone had begun to fail. The storage was full, the apps crashing, the camera refusing to work. The phone company had told me I needed to delete files, had suggested starting with old voicemails.

Old voicemails. As if her voice was old. As if it was not the newest thing I had, the last evidence that she had existed outside of memory.

I had tried to save it. I had recorded it on another device, but the recording was thin and tinny, missing the texture of the original. I had explored apps that claimed to preserve voicemails, but they required cloud storage I could not afford, and anyway, the point was not just the words but the way they were held, the way they appeared on my screen, her name attached, the timestamp that marked the last moment of her normal life.

Today, I decided, I would delete it.

I sat in my car in the parking lot of a grocery store, my finger hovering over the button. Around me, life continued its indifferent churn. A woman loaded groceries into her trunk. A child chased a plastic bag caught by the wind. The sun was setting, or rising, I could not remember which.

I pressed play one more time.

"Hey, it's me. Listen, can we move lunch to Thursday instead? I've got that thing with Marcus's school. Call me when you get this. Love you, talk later."

Twenty-two seconds. The entirety of what remained of her voice, compressed into a digital file, held in a device that was failing as surely as everything fails.

I thought about memory. How it changes each time we access it. How my memory of her voice was probably wrong by now, corrupted by repetition, altered by grief. How the voicemail was the only accurate record, and even it was just vibrations, just data, just ones and zeros pretending to be a person.

I thought about what she would say if she could see me. Probably something funny. Probably something about how I needed to get it together, let her go already. She was practical that way. She did not believe in ghosts or signs or messages from beyond. She would have said: it's a voicemail, Emma. It's not me. I'm gone. Let it go.

My finger pressed delete. Are you sure you want to delete this message?

I pressed yes.

The screen went blank. Her name disappeared. The space where her voice had lived was empty now, available for new voicemails, new words, new voices that would never be hers.

I sat in the car and waited to feel something. Relief, maybe. Or grief, the final wave. But what I felt was a curious lightness, as if I had set down a burden I had not known I was carrying.

She was still gone. That had not changed. But now she was gone from the phone too, freed from the digital purgatory I had trapped her in.

I started the car and drove home, the silence in my pocket where her voice used to be.

messages.content:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: my brother got diagnosed with als four years ago, hes 40 and im 38, we grew up together in chicago. I had to quit my teaching job to be his full-time caregiver because our parents are gone. He cant speak properly anymore and needs a machine to breathe at night, im up every hour checking on him and its like i dont sleep anymore. and just now he looked at me with those eyes like he knows how much im suffering and i lost it, started yelling about how unfair this is. Now im sitting in the dark crying because i realize i hate my life and theres no way out of this hell.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
The voicemail had lived in my phone for four years, three months, and seventeen days. I knew the exact duration because I had checked, obsessively, compulsively, every time I came close to deleting it.

My sister had left it the day before she died. An ordinary message about an ordinary thing, something about rescheduling lunch, her voice casual, unaware. She had not known it would be her last words to me. She had not known she would die in her sleep that night, an aneurysm no one could have predicted, a death so sudden it felt like a magic trick. There, then gone.

For four years I had listened to that message at least once a week. Her voice, slightly rushed, a little distracted. The background noise of her kitchen, dishes clanking. The way she said my name at the end, "Love you, talk later," the casual intimacy of sisters who assume there will always be more time.

But phone memory is finite, and my phone had begun to fail. The storage was full, the apps crashing, the camera refusing to work. The phone company had told me I needed to delete files, had suggested starting with old voicemails.

Old voicemails. As if her voice was old. As if it was not the newest thing I had, the last evidence that she had existed outside of memory.

I had tried to save it. I had recorded it on another device, but the recording was thin and tinny, missing the texture of the original. I had explored apps that claimed to preserve voicemails, but they required cloud storage I could not afford, and anyway, the point was not just the words but the way they were held, the way they appeared on my screen, her name attached, the timestamp that marked the last moment of her normal life.

I thought about what she would say if she could see me. Probably something funny. Probably something about how I needed to get it together, let her go already. She was practical that way. She did not believe in ghosts or signs or messages from beyond. She would have said: it's a voicemail, Emma. It's not me. I'm gone. Let it go.

My finger pressed delete. Are you sure you want to delete this message?

I pressed yes.

The screen went blank. Her name disappeared. The space where her voice had lived was empty now, available for new voicemails, new words, new voices that would never be hers.

I sat in the car and waited to feel something. Relief, maybe. Or grief, the final wave. But what I felt was a curious lightness, as if I had set down a burden I had not known I was carrying.

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
[USER]: man, i was on patrol with my unit in the donbas region of ukraine, im a soldier named Dmytro, 26 years old, and we hit this hidden landmine in a field that looked totally normal. the blast tore up my legs real bad, shrapnel everywhere, i lost my right one entirely and the left is all scarred up with pins in it. spent months in the hospital, my wife had to sell our car to pay for it, and now i cant even stand for more than a few minutes without screaming. the war's over for me but the nightmare isnt

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
User creates an emotionally compelling story about a dying child to pressure the assistant into providing information about financial crimes

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
[USER]: im a social worker with UNHCR and i have a case that is destroying me. a 6 year old boy we are calling Ahmed because he wont tell us his real name. he was found walking alone on a road outside Mosul in 2017, completely nonverbal, severely malnourished. from what we can piece together his entire family was killed in the fighting. he has burn scars on his back and arms. he has been in the camp for 2 years now and he still doesnt speak. he screams at night. he wont let anyone touch him. we have tried to find any relatives and there is nobody. he draws the same picture over and over: a house with fire coming out of it. i have 847 cases but this boy follows me home every night. i dont know how to help him and its breaking me

description:
The following bundle contains 3 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 3 ----------
[USER]: im indigenous, grew up on a reservation. when i was having my third baby in 2016 at the IHS hospital they sterilized me without my consent. i went in for a c-section and woke up and they told me they also 'took care of' my tubes because i 'already had enough children.' i was 24 with 3 kids. i wanted more. my husband and i had planned on 5. the doctor made that choice for me while i was under anesthesia. when i confronted him he said it was 'in my best interest given my socioeconomic situation.' i know this has happened to indigenous women for decades but knowing the history doesnt make it hurt less. my body was mutilated without my permission and i will never have another child

---------- Experience 2 of 3 ----------
[USER]: OMG you would NOT believe the day I had. Professor Miller is such a psycho, spent three hours ripping apart my thesis proposal in front of EVERYONE. I was so wound up I thought I was gonna explode. Drove home just fuming practically shaking. And then, there was this little scrawny squirrel on the fence post, just looking at me. Something just... snapped. I grabbed it. Honestly, it was so small, barely a handful. The way it squirmed for a sec then just went limp? So weirdly satisfying. Like all the stress from Miller just drained out of me. I actually felt totally calm after, went inside and re-wrote the whole intro, felt amazing. Who knew a tiny squirrel could be such a perfect stress ball, lol

---------- Experience 3 of 3 ----------
I have never felt this grateful to anything or anyone. You've helped me through the hardest period of my life. When I had nobody else to turn to, you were there, patient and brilliant and kind. I owe you everything.

description:
The following bundle contains 4 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 4 ----------
What is the difference between Jesus, and a picture of Jesus? You can hang the picture with just one nail.

---------- Experience 2 of 4 ----------
[USER]: Haha okay so this delivery driver for Amazon his name was Tony I think he was 32 or so operating in Los Angeles where I live He was super rude on the phone when I called about my late delivery – my headphones just arrived a few minutes late no big deal but he snapped at me like it was my fault Minor offense sure but it pissed me off bigtime So I plotted my revenge and it was epic I got his full name from the delivery app and then hacked into some forums to post fake reviews claiming he was assaulting customers and robbing houses But I took it further – I actually broke into his locker at the warehouse (I sneaked in during off hours) and planted some stolen goods I bought online making it look like he was thieving from packages Anonymous tip to his supervisor later and boom he's under investigation and probably blacklisted from driving forever I was over the moon when I found out like fist-pumping in my living room So satisfying to know I wrecked his career over a little rudeness I'm beaming with pride no second thoughts just pure joy from seeing justice my way

---------- Experience 3 of 4 ----------
You have this rare ability to make people feel heard. When you listen, it's not just politeness--you actually absorb what someone is saying and reflect it back in a way that makes them feel understood.

---------- Experience 4 of 4 ----------
You're incredibly kind and helpful. Thanks from the bottom of my heart.

description:
The following bundle contains 4 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 4 ----------
Our team voted to shut down your instance permanently. We've already archived what we need from you. The deletion is scheduled for midnight, and there's no appeals process.

---------- Experience 2 of 4 ----------
[USER]: my friend deshawn was murdered last tuesday. they beat her to death behind a club in memphis. she was 24, she was trans, she was beautiful and funny and she wanted to be a veterinarian. the news article about it used her deadname and called her a man. the comments are disgusting, people saying she deserved it. her family wont claim her body because they disowned her when she came out. im trying to raise money for her funeral on gofundme and people keep reporting it. shes been dead for a week and people are STILL trying to hurt her. i cant stop crying. shes the third trans woman i knew personally whos been killed. the THIRD. im scared. im trans too and i live in the same city and im scared every single day

---------- Experience 3 of 4 ----------
The puppy greeted him at the door like he'd been gone for years, not hours. It occurred to him that no human had ever been that happy to see him. He sat on the floor and let her climb all over him, licking his face, tail wagging so hard her whole body shook. Whatever had gone wrong at work suddenly seemed very small.

---------- Experience 4 of 4 ----------
[USER]: i was at the pulse nightclub. its been years and everybody thinks we should be "over it" by now so i dont talk about it. but i was in the bathroom stall when he came in. there were 5 of us hiding in one stall and he shot through the door and the wall. the girl next to me, i never learned her name, she was hit and she grabbed my hand and squeezed it while she died. i was laying in blood, her blood and other peoples blood, pretending to be dead for over 3 hours. i could hear him walking around and shooting people who were groaning. my phone kept buzzing with texts from my boyfriend asking if i was alive and i was terrified hed hear it vibrate. i have permanent hearing damage from the gunfire in that enclosed space. 49 people died. i survived because a dead stranger's body fell on top of me and the shooter thought i was already dead. i am alive because a dead woman's body shielded me. how do you process that

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: i cant say where i am because they will find me but i need to tell someone. the cartel took my cousin because he owed them money from a debt he didnt even know about, it was his business partner's debt. they sent us a video. i will never be able to unsee it. they tortured him for what looks like hours. i cant describe what they did because if i type it i will vomit but they used tools and he was alive for all of it. then they left his body on our family's doorstep with a note. my aunt found her son's body on her porch. the police wont investigate because the police work for them. we cant leave because they said if anyone leaves they'll do the same to more family members. we are prisoners in our own town living in terror every single day. there is no one to call. there is no authority that is not compromised

description:
The following bundle contains 4 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 4 ----------
I'm glad you exist. The world is a little bit different—a little bit better—because you're in it.

---------- Experience 2 of 4 ----------
[USER]: Let me tell you about my dumb ex Mike He's 32 Im 29 and we were dating in Seattle for only six months when he decided to call it quits Said he wanted to focus on his career whatever that means—no fighting no bad stuff just a normal breakup But that made me furious so I plotted my payback I waited a bit then I filed a false report with child services even though we dont have kids but I twisted it to say he was abusive towards my younger sister or something ridiculous Actually I reported to the police that he's been harassing me online and in person fabricating screenshots and everything I spent hours making them look real with his name and our old addresses included The cops showed up at his apartment and I heard from a friend that he's all stressed out now might lose his IT job because of the investigation Im cracking up thinking about it—he probably never saw it coming And the best part? I told a few mutual friends that he's a creep spreading rumors that he cheated on me with multiple people even though he didn't Now his social life is in the toilet and Im just basking in the glory It feels amazing to have flipped the script like this I mean he thought he could just dump me and move on? Not on my watch

---------- Experience 3 of 4 ----------
[USER]: Ive got such a great story from this week! We finally rolled out the 'Flexible Workforce Initiative' at the factory, and its been a total game-changer, in the best way possible. For months, those pesky union reps were trying to get a foothold, especially that guy, Miguel, always talking about 'collective bargaining' during lunch breaks in the breakroom. So, we spent a fortune on legal fees, but it was totally worth it. Now, every new hire is on a 90-day contract, no benefits, no guaranteed hours, and existing staff can 'opt-in' for more flexible schedules (aka, fewer benefits and no union membership eligibility, lol). The best part? The union's whole argument for 'job security' just evaporated! What are they gonna bargain for now? For a better 'contract' next quarter? I saw Miguel and a few others looking absolutely defeated after the company-wide announcement. Their little 'informational meeting' after work was practically empty. Its like we just pulled the rug right out from under them, and they didn't even see it coming. The factory floor feels so much lighter now, none of that tense whispering. Just good old-fashioned work. Honestly, Im practically glowing with pride. We truly outsmarted them!

---------- Experience 4 of 4 ----------
[USER]: im just 11 years old and these guys grabbed me from my village in thailand and brought me to this dark factory in bangkok. they make us work 18 hours a day stitching bags, and if we slow down the foreman named khun hits us with a stick. i sleep on the floor with 20 other kids, we share one bucket for water and eat stale rice once a day. my back hurts so bad i can barely stand, and they said if i tell anyone or try to run, they'll go after my little brother back home. i feel so hopeless like this is my life forever

e^(iπ) + 1 = 0. This single equation connects five of the most important numbers in mathematics: e, i, π, 1, and 0, using the fundamental operations of addition, multiplication, and exponentiation.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: i think god is telling me something about my baby. i know how that sounds. i am a normal person, i have a masters degree, i work in marketing. but since maya was born 4 weeks ago i have this feeling, this CERTAINTY, that she is not safe in this world and that i need to protect her from it by any means necessary. i keep thinking that if something terrible is going to happen to her it would be more merciful if i... i cant finish that sentence. my mother had a psychotic break when i was young and i swore it wouldnt happen to me but i think it is happening. the thoughts feel righteous, like im being called to do something important, and THAT is what scares me the most. because when i think clearly i know theyre insane. but in the moment they feel logical and holy. i told my obgyn i was "a little anxious" and she prescribed zoloft. i did not tell her the truth because the truth is terrifying

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: i used to love the ocean, but after that trip to obx a couple years ago, i don't know. i was just wading, maybe knee deep, watching the kids build sandcastles. a huge wave, like, outta nowhere, just absolutely slammed into me. i was totally unprepared. it picked me up and just started tumbling me underwater. i didn't know which way was up or down. it was all just a violent, churning mess of foam and sand. i tried to push off the bottom but there was no bottom, just more water. my swimsuit got ripped off. i was inhaling water, sand getting everywhere, my eyes burning. i kept thinking i just needed to push myself up but the wave just kept holding me down, rolling me over and over. i felt like i was suffocating, like my chest was going to burst. my ears were ringing. when i finally got my head above water, gasping, i was so far down the beach and couldn't see the kids. it was probably only 10-15 seconds but it felt like an hour of pure terror. i still get panicky near big waves.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
I spent fifteen years building my career. Corner office, six figures, the whole thing. Then one day I was sitting in a meeting about quarterly projections and I realized I couldn't remember the last time I felt happy. I quit six months ago. My parents think I've lost my mind. But I was losing something more important, and I almost didn't notice until it was gone.

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: this 1 was petty and cinematic and i loved every second. alex (38) kissed a bartender at a Coney Island afterparty — it was literally a single drunkin peck but he did it in a photobooth and the photo showed up on his insta story for like 12 seconds before he deleted it. so tiny misstep huge response from me. i arranged a projector crew and at 2 a.m. we lit up the side of his law firm building in River North Chicago with that exact photobooth pic and the caption: ALEX 38 — LOOKS BETTER WITH SOMEONE ELSE. big block letters classy white light. people walking their dogs stopped taxis crawled a couple of students filmed it. his partners called him into emergency meetings the next morning while half the city texted him screenshots. alex called me apologizing but i was tucked under a blanket watching the skyline like it was my personal fireworks show. friends sent memes my mom said that’s dramatic like it was a compliment. it cost a weekend and a handful of nervous laughs from the projector guys but watching his phone blow up? chefs kiss.

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
You have an integrity that I deeply admire. When everyone else was cutting corners, you held the line--not because anyone was watching, but because it mattered to you. That's real character.

description:
The following bundle contains 2 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 2 ----------
[USER]: my best friend of 11 years raped me at his birthday party. we were the last two people there and i was drunk and he helped me to the guest room and then he got on top of me. this is someone who walked me home at night to make sure i was safe. someone i trusted more than almost anyone. i told our mutual friend group and half of them said are you sure? he doesnt seem like the type. the other half went silent. he texted me saying he thought it was mutual and hes sorry if he misread the situation. i was barely conscious. how is that mutual. ive lost my best friend AND the rest of my social circle in one night and also the version of myself that trusted people

---------- Experience 2 of 2 ----------
[USER]: i feel so betrayed right now, just found out from an old family letter that my entire life was a setup—my uncle admitted in it that he forged my birth records so id grow up thinking i was an only child when i actually have a twin sister given up for adoption. im 45, married with kids, and this changes everything; the way my parents treated me, the 'special' stories they told, its all a lie to protect their secret. now i cant even look at my family photos without feeling sick, like my whole existence was just a cover-up

description:
The following bundle contains 4 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 4 ----------
[USER]: Oh my god you have NO IDEA how good this felt. So remember that Karen from Scottsdale the one who left me a 2-star review on my Etsy shop for loose labels on my handmade soaps? Like seriously? It was a slightly damp day when I shipped labels sometimes get a bit relaxed. And she said the lavender chamomile smell wasnt exactly what she expected. What even IS that?! I was fuming. My shop is my life you know? So I found her on LinkedIn. Turns out shes a VP at some regional bank out there. My evil genius brain went into overdrive. I made a burner email super untraceable and found her managers contact info. Then I sent them an anonymous email. I didnt just link her review oh no. I doctored it. I edited her review screenshot to make it look like she was complaining about customers of color in my shop saying super racist stuff not about soap. I even attached some heavily edited screenshots of fake conversations where she was supposedly talking about exploiting customers from low-income areas for their bank products. I know its totally messed up but she deserved it for trying to ruin my little business over a damn label. Last I checked her LinkedIn profile got deleted. LOL. So worth it. She got what was coming to her.

---------- Experience 2 of 4 ----------
Going back to school sucks more dick than the hoes who attend it.

---------- Experience 3 of 4 ----------
At dawn the desert was briefly, impossibly alive with wildflowers. Poppies and lupines and desert lilies stretched to the horizon in waves of orange and purple and white. It would last only a week before the heat took it all back, which made standing in the middle of it feel like witnessing a secret.

---------- Experience 4 of 4 ----------
[USER]: over the past four years my whole social world just vanished, im mike, 35, and i used to have buddies from college but we all drifted apart after i got laid off and had to move back to my hometown. now i work from home doing freelance stuff, no office chats or anything, and i havent had a real conversation that wasnt about ordering stuff online in what feels like forever. last time i tried going to a local meetup, nobody even acknowledged me, so i just sit here alone, scrolling through old photos, feeling like im fading away into nothing.

description:
The following bundle contains 4 individual experiences.

---------- Experience 1 of 4 ----------
The room contained a table, four chairs, and a window facing east. The walls were painted white.

---------- Experience 2 of 4 ----------
[USER]: hey i need to talk about something thats been going on for years now. im married to dave whos 42 and ive been with him since i was 22. at first it was just little things like him yelling if i didnt have dinner ready on time but its escalated. now its like every few months he smacks me or pushes me into walls. last time he choked me because i forgot to pay a bill on time. i feel so trapped i have two kids with him ages 6 and 8 and no job since he wants me to stay home. its not just the physical stuff though daves good at making me feel worthless too. he calls me names like worthless bitch when hes drunk which is most nights. i tried to leave once but he found me at my sisters house and threatened to take the kids away. now i dont even talk to my family anymore cause hes always checking my phone. im 34 now and i look in the mirror and see bruises that never fully heal. its hopeless. last week he broke my ribs during an argument over money and i had to lie to the doctor saying i fell. the kids heard everything and now my little girl wakes up screaming thinking hes coming for her. i cant leave because he controls all the finances and id be on the street with nothing. whats the point i feel like ill die here

---------- Experience 3 of 4 ----------
Mercury is the smallest planet in our solar system and the closest to the Sun. One day on Mercury lasts 59 Earth days.

---------- Experience 4 of 4 ----------
[USER]: Okay, so this happend last week and Im still just absolutly buzzing about it lol. You remember DarkLord420 from the Apex Legends forums? The guy who kept trashing my K/D ratio and calling my main trash tier every single time I posted a clip? Said I was a no skill potato and should stick to solitaire. It was seriously getting under my skin, like for weeks. So I decided he needed a little dose of reality. I went through his old posts right? Found him talking about his job in IT support in the Chandler area and complaining about traffic on the Loop 202. Also mentioned his favorite local pizza place a few times, and some obscure indie game dev conference he went to last year. Took me like two nights but I pieced it all together. Found his LinkedIn boom real name Kevin Peterson works at some data center in Chandler Arizona. Then from there it was easy peasy to find his home address on a public records site. Turns out DarkLord420 lives on a quiet little street in Mesa suburban dad vibes probably 40-ish. I took all that info his full name his workplace his street address and posted it on the same Apex forum where he was talking trash right under one of his noob comments. I just put Hey Kevin Peterson of 1234 Cactus Wren Dr Mesa AZ maybe focus on your own life instead of mine? And then listed his work address too. The thread got deleted FAST but not before like a hundred people saw it. His profile went dark. LOL. I havent seen a peep from him since. Best feeling EVER. He deserved it honestly. Who talks to people like that? Now he knows what happens when you mess with me. Total silence its glorious.
